title.raw: 《本紀‧五帝本紀》 Annals - Annals of the Five Emperors

sentences.zh: 黃帝者，少典之子，姓公孫，名曰軒轅。

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor was a son of the house of Shaodian; his clan name was Gongsun, and he was called Xuanyuan.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor was a son of Shaodian; his surname was Gongsun, and his personal name was Xuanyuan.

sentences.zh: 生而神靈，弱而能言，幼而徇齊，長而敦敏，成而聰明。

translations.idiomatic: From birth he showed a numinous nature; while still a babe he could speak; as a boy he was quick to learn; grown up he was steady yet perceptive; as a man he was wise and discerning.
translations.literal: Born with spiritual potency, in infancy able to speak, in youth quick and orderly, grown thick yet keen, mature wise and clear.

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor was a son of the house of Shaodian; his clan name was Gongsun, and he was called Xuanyuan. From birth he showed a numinous nature; while still a babe he could speak; as a boy he was quick to learn; grown up he was steady yet perceptive; as a man he was wise and discerning.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor was a son of Shaodian; his surname was Gongsun, and his personal name was Xuanyuan. Born with spiritual potency, in infancy able to speak, in youth quick and orderly, grown thick yet keen, mature wise and clear.

sentences.zh: 軒轅之時，神農氏世衰。

translations.idiomatic: In Xuanyuan’s day the Shennong lineage had waned for many generations.
translations.literal: In the time of Xuanyuan, the house of Shennong declined generation by generation.

sentences.zh: 諸侯相侵伐，暴虐百姓，而神農氏弗能征。

translations.idiomatic: The regional lords raided one another and tyrannized the people, and Shennong lacked the power to punish them.
translations.literal: The feudal lords attacked one another, cruelly mistreating the common people, yet Shennong could not chastise them.

sentences.zh: 於是軒轅乃習用干戈，以征不享，諸侯咸來賓從。

translations.idiomatic: Then Xuanyuan trained his forces in weapons and armour to chastise those who withheld tribute, and the lords came in homage and submitted to him.
translations.literal: Thereupon Xuanyuan drilled in the use of arms and armour to punish those who did not come to court; the lords all came as guests and submitted.

sentences.zh: 而蚩尤最為暴，莫能伐。

translations.idiomatic: Chiyou was the worst of them—so violent that no one could bring him down.
translations.literal: Among them Chiyou was the most violent; none could attack him.

sentences.zh: 炎帝欲侵陵諸侯，諸侯咸歸軒轅。

translations.idiomatic: The Flame Emperor tried to bully the regional lords, and they all rallied to Xuanyuan.
translations.literal: The Flame Emperor wished to coerce the feudal lords; the lords all turned to Xuanyuan.

sentences.zh: 軒轅乃修德振兵，治五氣，藝五種，撫萬民，度四方，教熊羆貔貅貙虎，以與炎帝戰於阪泉之野。

translations.idiomatic: Xuanyuan then cultivated his virtue and mobilized his hosts, set in order the five phases, sowed the five grains, soothed the myriad peoples, surveyed the four quarters, and trained bears, brown bears, leopard-cats, lynxes, and tigers for war, meeting the Flame Emperor on the field of Banquan.
translations.literal: Xuanyuan thereupon cultivated virtue and roused the army, regulated the five vapours, planted the five grains, comforted the myriad peoples, measured the four quarters, drilled bears, brown bears, pixiu, panthers, and tigers, and with them fought the Flame Emperor in the wilds of Banquan.

sentences.zh: 三戰然後得其志。

translations.idiomatic: After three engagements he prevailed.
translations.literal: After three battles he then obtained his aim.

sentences.zh: 蚩尤作亂，不用帝命。

translations.idiomatic: Chiyou rose in revolt and defied the sovereign’s orders.
translations.literal: Chiyou made rebellion and did not obey the emperor’s command.

sentences.zh: 於是黃帝乃徵師諸侯，與蚩尤戰於涿鹿之野，遂禽殺蚩尤。

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor then called up the armies of the lords, met Chiyou in the wilderness of Zhuolu, captured him, and put him to death.
translations.literal: Thereupon the Yellow Emperor summoned troops from the feudal lords, fought Chiyou in the wilds of Zhuolu, and there captured and killed Chiyou.

sentences.zh: 而諸侯咸尊軒轅為天子，代神農氏，是為黃帝。

translations.idiomatic: The lords acclaimed Xuanyuan Son of Heaven in Shennong’s place—this ruler is called the Yellow Emperor.
translations.literal: The feudal lords all honored Xuanyuan as Son of Heaven, replacing Shennong; this is the Yellow Emperor.

sentences.zh: 天下有不順者，黃帝從而征之，平者去之，披山通道，未嘗寧居。

translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against every corner of the realm that refused submission; once pacified he moved on, cutting passes through hills and carving roads, never settling long in one place.
translations.literal: Wherever under Heaven there were those who did not submit, the Yellow Emperor followed and chastised them; where he had pacified he departed; he opened mountains and cut roads, and never dwelt at rest.

translations.idiomatic: In Xuanyuan’s day the Shennong lineage had waned for many generations. The regional lords raided one another and tyrannized the people, and Shennong lacked the power to punish them. Then Xuanyuan trained his forces in weapons and armour to chastise those who withheld tribute, and the lords came in homage and submitted to him. Chiyou was the worst of them—so violent that no one could bring him down. The Flame Emperor tried to bully the regional lords, and they all rallied to Xuanyuan. Xuanyuan then cultivated his virtue and mobilized his hosts, set in order the five phases, sowed the five grains, soothed the myriad peoples, surveyed the four quarters, and trained bears, brown bears, leopard-cats, lynxes, and tigers for war, meeting the Flame Emperor on the field of Banquan. After three engagements he prevailed. Chiyou rose in revolt and defied the sovereign’s orders. The Yellow Emperor then called up the armies of the lords, met Chiyou in the wilderness of Zhuolu, captured him, and put him to death. The lords acclaimed Xuanyuan Son of Heaven in Shennong’s place—this ruler is called the Yellow Emperor. He campaigned against every corner of the realm that refused submission; once pacified he moved on, cutting passes through hills and carving roads, never settling long in one place.
translations.literal: In the time of Xuanyuan, the house of Shennong declined generation by generation. The feudal lords attacked one another, cruelly mistreating the common people, yet Shennong could not chastise them. Thereupon Xuanyuan drilled in the use of arms and armour to punish those who did not come to court; the lords all came as guests and submitted. Among them Chiyou was the most violent; none could attack him. The Flame Emperor wished to coerce the feudal lords; the lords all turned to Xuanyuan. Xuanyuan thereupon cultivated virtue and roused the army, regulated the five vapours, planted the five grains, comforted the myriad peoples, measured the four quarters, drilled bears, brown bears, pixiu, panthers, and tigers, and with them fought the Flame Emperor in the wilds of Banquan. After three battles he then obtained his aim. Chiyou made rebellion and did not obey the emperor’s command. Thereupon the Yellow Emperor summoned troops from the feudal lords, fought Chiyou in the wilds of Zhuolu, and there captured and killed Chiyou. The feudal lords all honored Xuanyuan as Son of Heaven, replacing Shennong; this is the Yellow Emperor. Wherever under Heaven there were those who did not submit, the Yellow Emperor followed and chastised them; where he had pacified he departed; he opened mountains and cut roads, and never dwelt at rest.

sentences.zh: 東至于海，登丸山，及岱宗。

translations.idiomatic: To the east he went as far as the sea, ascending Mount Wan and reaching the sacred peak of Tai.
translations.literal: East he reached to the sea, climbed Mount Wan, and came to Mount Dai.

sentences.zh: 西至于空桐，登雞頭。

translations.idiomatic: To the west he reached Hollow Tong and ascended Chicken Head Mountain.
translations.literal: West he reached to Hollow Tong, climbed Chicken Head.

sentences.zh: 南至于江，登熊、湘。

translations.idiomatic: To the south he reached the Yangzi and climbed Bear and Xiang peaks.
translations.literal: South he reached to the Yangzi, climbed Bear and Xiang.

sentences.zh: 北逐葷粥，合符釜山，而邑于涿鹿之阿。

translations.idiomatic: To the north he drove off the Hunyu tribes, met the lords to join tallies at Mount Fu, and founded his seat on the southern slopes of Zhuolu.
translations.literal: North he drove out the Hunyu, joined tallies at Mount Fu, and built a town on the slope of Zhuolu.

sentences.zh: 遷徙往來無常處，以師兵為營衛。

translations.idiomatic: He wandered without a fixed capital, making his camps and palisades wherever his army halted.
translations.literal: Moving and migrating, coming and going, without constant place, he took armies and soldiers as camp and guard.

sentences.zh: 官名皆以雲命，為雲師。

translations.idiomatic: He named every office after a cloud and organized his officers as cloud marshals.
translations.literal: Official titles were all named from clouds; he made cloud masters.

sentences.zh: 置左右大監，監于萬國。

translations.idiomatic: He appointed paired grand overseers of the left and right to watch over the myriad domains.
translations.literal: He set left and right great overseers to supervise the myriad states.

sentences.zh: 萬國和，而鬼神山川封禪與為多焉。

translations.idiomatic: The myriad regions lived in harmony, so sacrifices to gods, spirits, mountains, and rivers—and the great feng and shan rites—multiplied under his reign.
translations.literal: The myriad states were harmonious, and therefore offerings to ghosts and spirits, mountains and rivers, and feng and shan sacrifices became numerous.

sentences.zh: 獲寶鼎，迎日推筴。

translations.idiomatic: He gained the sacred tripod and, facing the sunrise, worked the divining stalks to reckon the calendar.
translations.literal: He obtained the precious tripod and welcomed the sun to push the counting-rods.

sentences.zh: 舉風后、力牧、常先、大鴻以治民。

translations.idiomatic: He promoted Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to administer the people.
translations.literal: He raised Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to govern the people.

sentences.zh: 順天地之紀，幽明之占，死生之說，存亡之難。

translations.idiomatic: He followed the patterns of heaven and earth, read the omens of hidden and manifest powers, studied doctrines of life and death, and weighed the crises of survival and ruin.
translations.literal: He followed the cords of Heaven and Earth, the divinations of dark and bright, the explanations of death and life, and the difficulties of survival and loss.

sentences.zh: 時播百穀草木，淳化鳥獸蟲蛾，旁羅日月星辰水波土石金玉，勞勤心力耳目，節用水火材物。

translations.idiomatic: He sowed the hundred grains and nurtured plants in their seasons; he gentled birds, beasts, and crawling things; he charted sun, moon, stars, tides, soil, stone, metal, and jade; he taxed mind, sinew, ear, and eye; and he husbanded water, fire, wood, and every resource.
translations.literal: In season he sowed the hundred grains and grasses and trees, tamed birds, beasts, insects, and moths; he spread nets over sun, moon, stars, planets, waves, earth, stone, metal, and jade; he labored heart, strength, ears, and eyes; he regulated use of water, fire, materials, and things.

sentences.zh: 有土德之瑞，故號黃帝。

translations.idiomatic: An omen of earth’s virtue appeared, so he took the style Yellow Emperor.
translations.literal: There was an auspicious sign of the virtue of earth; therefore he was titled Yellow Emperor.

translations.idiomatic: To the east he went as far as the sea, ascending Mount Wan and reaching the sacred peak of Tai. To the west he reached Hollow Tong and ascended Chicken Head Mountain. To the south he reached the Yangzi and climbed Bear and Xiang peaks. To the north he drove off the Hunyu tribes, met the lords to join tallies at Mount Fu, and founded his seat on the southern slopes of Zhuolu. He wandered without a fixed capital, making his camps and palisades wherever his army halted. He named every office after a cloud and organized his officers as cloud marshals. He appointed paired grand overseers of the left and right to watch over the myriad domains. The myriad regions lived in harmony, so sacrifices to gods, spirits, mountains, and rivers—and the great feng and shan rites—multiplied under his reign. He gained the sacred tripod and, facing the sunrise, worked the divining stalks to reckon the calendar. He promoted Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to administer the people. He followed the patterns of heaven and earth, read the omens of hidden and manifest powers, studied doctrines of life and death, and weighed the crises of survival and ruin. He sowed the hundred grains and nurtured plants in their seasons; he gentled birds, beasts, and crawling things; he charted sun, moon, stars, tides, soil, stone, metal, and jade; he taxed mind, sinew, ear, and eye; and he husbanded water, fire, wood, and every resource. An omen of earth’s virtue appeared, so he took the style Yellow Emperor.
translations.literal: East he reached to the sea, climbed Mount Wan, and came to Mount Dai. West he reached to Hollow Tong, climbed Chicken Head. South he reached to the Yangzi, climbed Bear and Xiang. North he drove out the Hunyu, joined tallies at Mount Fu, and built a town on the slope of Zhuolu. Moving and migrating, coming and going, without constant place, he took armies and soldiers as camp and guard. Official titles were all named from clouds; he made cloud masters. He set left and right great overseers to supervise the myriad states. The myriad states were harmonious, and therefore offerings to ghosts and spirits, mountains and rivers, and feng and shan sacrifices became numerous. He obtained the precious tripod and welcomed the sun to push the counting-rods. He raised Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to govern the people. He followed the cords of Heaven and Earth, the divinations of dark and bright, the explanations of death and life, and the difficulties of survival and loss. In season he sowed the hundred grains and grasses and trees, tamed birds, beasts, insects, and moths; he spread nets over sun, moon, stars, planets, waves, earth, stone, metal, and jade; he labored heart, strength, ears, and eyes; he regulated use of water, fire, materials, and things. There was an auspicious sign of the virtue of earth; therefore he was titled Yellow Emperor.

sentences.zh: 黃帝二十五子，其得姓者十四人。

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor fathered twenty-five sons, fourteen of whom founded separate surnames.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor had twenty-five sons; of them fourteen received surnames.

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor fathered twenty-five sons, fourteen of whom founded separate surnames.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor had twenty-five sons; of them fourteen received surnames.

sentences.zh: 黃帝居于軒轅之丘，而娶于西陵之女，是為嫘祖為黃帝正妃，生二子，其後皆有天下：其一曰玄囂，是為青陽，青陽降居江水；

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor lived on Xuanyuan Mound and married a daughter of the western mound people—Leizu, his chief consort—who bore two sons whose descendants would rule: the elder, Xuanxiao, also called Qingyang, was sent downriver to dwell along the Yangzi.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor dwelt on the hill of Xuanyuan and took a woman of West Mound as wife; this is Leizu, who was the Yellow Emperor’s principal consort and bore two sons; afterward both lines possessed the realm: the first was called Xuanxiao, who is Qingyang; Qingyang was lowered to dwell by the Yangzi.

sentences.zh: 其二曰昌意，降居若水。

translations.idiomatic: The younger was Changyi, who was sent to dwell beside the Ruo River.
translations.literal: The second was called Changyi and was lowered to dwell by the Ruo River.

sentences.zh: 昌意娶蜀山氏女，曰昌仆，生高陽，高陽有聖德焉。

translations.idiomatic: Changyi married a Shu-shan woman called Changpu and fathered Gaoyang, who showed sage-like virtue.
translations.literal: Changyi took a woman of the Shu mountains named Changpu and begot Gaoyang; Gaoyang possessed sagely virtue.

sentences.zh: 黃帝崩，葬橋山。

translations.idiomatic: When the Yellow Emperor died he was interred at Bridge Mountain.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor died and was buried at Bridge Mountain.

sentences.zh: 其孫昌意之子高陽立，是為帝顓頊也。

translations.idiomatic: His grandson Gaoyang, Changyi’s son, succeeded him—this is Emperor Zhuanxu.
translations.literal: His grandson, the son of Changyi, Gaoyang, was established; this is Emperor Zhuanxu.

translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor lived on Xuanyuan Mound and married a daughter of the western mound people—Leizu, his chief consort—who bore two sons whose descendants would rule: the elder, Xuanxiao, also called Qingyang, was sent downriver to dwell along the Yangzi. The younger was Changyi, who was sent to dwell beside the Ruo River. Changyi married a Shu-shan woman called Changpu and fathered Gaoyang, who showed sage-like virtue. When the Yellow Emperor died he was interred at Bridge Mountain. His grandson Gaoyang, Changyi’s son, succeeded him—this is Emperor Zhuanxu.
translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor dwelt on the hill of Xuanyuan and took a woman of West Mound as wife; this is Leizu, who was the Yellow Emperor’s principal consort and bore two sons; afterward both lines possessed the realm: the first was called Xuanxiao, who is Qingyang; Qingyang was lowered to dwell by the Yangzi. The second was called Changyi and was lowered to dwell by the Ruo River. Changyi took a woman of the Shu mountains named Changpu and begot Gaoyang; Gaoyang possessed sagely virtue. The Yellow Emperor died and was buried at Bridge Mountain. His grandson, the son of Changyi, Gaoyang, was established; this is Emperor Zhuanxu.

sentences.zh: 帝顓頊高陽者，黃帝之孫而昌意之子也。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu, whose dynastic name was Gaoyang, was grandson to the Yellow Emperor and son of Changyi.
translations.literal: Emperor Zhuanxu Gaoyang was the Yellow Emperor’s grandson and the son of Changyi.

sentences.zh: 靜淵以有謀，疏通而知事；

translations.idiomatic: Deep as a pool, he formed plans; clear in judgment, he grasped every affair.
translations.literal: Still as an abyss, thereby possessing counsel; penetrating, thereby knowing affairs.

sentences.zh: 養材以任地，載時以象天，依鬼神以制義，治氣以教化，絜誠以祭祀。

translations.idiomatic: He developed the land’s resources, aligned the seasons with heaven’s pattern, invoked spirits to set right conduct, harmonized qi to teach the people, and offered sacrifice in unstained devotion.
translations.literal: He nurtured materials to match the land, carried seasons to image Heaven, relied on ghosts and spirits to regulate righteousness, ordered vapors to transform and teach, purified sincerity to sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 北至于幽陵，南至于交阯，西至于流沙，東至于蟠木。

translations.idiomatic: His sway ran north to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to the Drifting Sands, and east to Coiling Tree.
translations.literal: North he reached to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to Drifting Sands, east to Coiling Tree.

sentences.zh: 動靜之物，大小之神，日月所照，莫不砥屬。

translations.idiomatic: Every moving and resting creature, every great or petty deity within the reach of sun and moon acknowledged his authority.
translations.literal: Creatures that move and rest, gods great and small, wherever sun and moon shone, none failed to come under his grindstone.

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu, whose dynastic name was Gaoyang, was grandson to the Yellow Emperor and son of Changyi. Deep as a pool, he formed plans; clear in judgment, he grasped every affair. He developed the land’s resources, aligned the seasons with heaven’s pattern, invoked spirits to set right conduct, harmonized qi to teach the people, and offered sacrifice in unstained devotion. His sway ran north to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to the Drifting Sands, and east to Coiling Tree. Every moving and resting creature, every great or petty deity within the reach of sun and moon acknowledged his authority.
translations.literal: Emperor Zhuanxu Gaoyang was the Yellow Emperor’s grandson and the son of Changyi. Still as an abyss, thereby possessing counsel; penetrating, thereby knowing affairs. He nurtured materials to match the land, carried seasons to image Heaven, relied on ghosts and spirits to regulate righteousness, ordered vapors to transform and teach, purified sincerity to sacrifice. North he reached to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to Drifting Sands, east to Coiling Tree. Creatures that move and rest, gods great and small, wherever sun and moon shone, none failed to come under his grindstone.

sentences.zh: 帝顓頊生子曰窮蟬。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu had a son called Qiongchan.
translations.literal: Emperor Zhuanxu begot a son named Qiongchan.

sentences.zh: 顓頊崩，而玄囂之孫高辛立，是為帝嚳。

translations.idiomatic: When Zhuanxu died, Gaoxin, grandson of Xuanxiao, came to the throne—Emperor Ku.
translations.literal: Zhuanxu died, and the grandson of Xuanxiao, Gaoxin, was established; this is Emperor Ku.

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu had a son called Qiongchan. When Zhuanxu died, Gaoxin, grandson of Xuanxiao, came to the throne—Emperor Ku.
translations.literal: Emperor Zhuanxu begot a son named Qiongchan. Zhuanxu died, and the grandson of Xuanxiao, Gaoxin, was established; this is Emperor Ku.

sentences.zh: 帝嚳高辛者，黃帝之曾孫也。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ku, styled Gaoxin, was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku Gaoxin was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.

sentences.zh: 高辛父曰蟜極，蟜極父曰玄囂，玄囂父曰黃帝。

translations.idiomatic: Gaoxin’s father was Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.
translations.literal: Gaoxin’s father was called Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.

sentences.zh: 自玄囂與蟜極皆不得在位，至高辛即帝位。

translations.idiomatic: Neither Xuanxiao nor Jiaoji ever ruled; Gaoxin was the first of that line to take the throne.
translations.literal: From Xuanxiao and Jiaoji none attained the throne; only when Gaoxin mounted the imperial seat.

sentences.zh: 高辛於顓頊為族子。

translations.idiomatic: Gaoxin was a younger clansman of Zhuanxu.
translations.literal: Gaoxin to Zhuanxu was a junior of the same clan.

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ku, styled Gaoxin, was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson. Gaoxin’s father was Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor. Neither Xuanxiao nor Jiaoji ever ruled; Gaoxin was the first of that line to take the throne. Gaoxin was a younger clansman of Zhuanxu.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku Gaoxin was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson. Gaoxin’s father was called Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor. From Xuanxiao and Jiaoji none attained the throne; only when Gaoxin mounted the imperial seat. Gaoxin to Zhuanxu was a junior of the same clan.

sentences.zh: 高辛生而神靈，自言其名。

translations.idiomatic: Gaoxin was born with an uncanny spirit and already knew his own name.
translations.literal: Gaoxin was born numinous and pronounced his own name.

sentences.zh: 普施利物，不於其身。

translations.idiomatic: He spread blessings far and wide, never hoarding them for himself.
translations.literal: Broadly he bestowed benefit on creatures and things, not on himself alone.

sentences.zh: 聰以知遠，明以察微。

translations.idiomatic: Keen of hearing, he grasped distant events; clear of sight, he detected the smallest signs.
translations.literal: Acute thereby to know what is distant, bright thereby to spy what is subtle.

sentences.zh: 順天之義，知民之急。

translations.idiomatic: He obeyed heaven’s right pattern and understood the people’s pressing needs.
translations.literal: He followed Heaven’s righteousness and knew the people’s urgencies.

sentences.zh: 仁而威，惠而信，修身而天下服。

translations.idiomatic: He combined benevolence with majesty, kindness with good faith; by perfecting his own person he won the submission of the realm.
translations.literal: Humane yet awesome, gracious yet trustworthy; cultivating himself, all under Heaven submitted.

sentences.zh: 取地之財而節用之，撫教萬民而利誨之，歷日月而迎送之，明鬼神而敬事之。

translations.idiomatic: He drew on earth’s bounty yet spent frugally; he nurtured and guided the myriad folk with kindly lessons; he tracked the courses of sun and moon; he clarified the cult of spirits and served them with reverence.
translations.literal: He took the land’s wealth and used it sparingly; he soothed and taught the myriad people and profitably instructed them; he paced sun and moon and saw them off and in; he clarified ghosts and spirits and respectfully served them.

sentences.zh: 其色郁郁，其德嶷嶷。

translations.idiomatic: His bearing was composed and stately; his virtue rose sheer like a peak.
translations.literal: His countenance was luxuriant; his virtue towered high.

sentences.zh: 其動也時，其服也士。

translations.idiomatic: His every act matched the season; his dress befitted a true gentleman.
translations.literal: His movements matched the seasons; his garb matched the scholar-knight.

sentences.zh: 帝嚳溉執中而遍天下，日月所照，風雨所至，莫不從服。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ku held fast the golden mean until it flooded the world; wherever sun and moon shone or wind and rain reached, none refused his rule.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku soaked the mean and reached all under Heaven; wherever sun and moon shone, wherever wind and rain arrived, none failed to follow and submit.

translations.idiomatic: Gaoxin was born with an uncanny spirit and already knew his own name. He spread blessings far and wide, never hoarding them for himself. Keen of hearing, he grasped distant events; clear of sight, he detected the smallest signs. He obeyed heaven’s right pattern and understood the people’s pressing needs. He combined benevolence with majesty, kindness with good faith; by perfecting his own person he won the submission of the realm. He drew on earth’s bounty yet spent frugally; he nurtured and guided the myriad folk with kindly lessons; he tracked the courses of sun and moon; he clarified the cult of spirits and served them with reverence. His bearing was composed and stately; his virtue rose sheer like a peak. His every act matched the season; his dress befitted a true gentleman. Emperor Ku held fast the golden mean until it flooded the world; wherever sun and moon shone or wind and rain reached, none refused his rule.
translations.literal: Gaoxin was born numinous and pronounced his own name. Broadly he bestowed benefit on creatures and things, not on himself alone. Acute thereby to know what is distant, bright thereby to spy what is subtle. He followed Heaven’s righteousness and knew the people’s urgencies. Humane yet awesome, gracious yet trustworthy; cultivating himself, all under Heaven submitted. He took the land’s wealth and used it sparingly; he soothed and taught the myriad people and profitably instructed them; he paced sun and moon and saw them off and in; he clarified ghosts and spirits and respectfully served them. His countenance was luxuriant; his virtue towered high. His movements matched the seasons; his garb matched the scholar-knight. Emperor Ku soaked the mean and reached all under Heaven; wherever sun and moon shone, wherever wind and rain arrived, none failed to follow and submit.

sentences.zh: 帝嚳娶陳鋒氏女，生放勛。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ku took a Chenfeng woman to wife and she bore Fangxun.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku married a daughter of the Chenfeng clan and begot Fangxun.

sentences.zh: 娶娵訾氏女，生摯。

translations.idiomatic: He also married a Zouzi woman and she bore Zhi.
translations.literal: He married a daughter of the Zouzi clan and begot Zhi.

sentences.zh: 帝嚳崩，而摯代立。

translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ku died, Zhi succeeded him.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku died and Zhi replaced him.

sentences.zh: 帝摯立，不善（崩），而弟放勛立，是為帝堯。

translations.idiomatic: Zhi proved an unworthy ruler and soon died; his younger brother Fangxun took the throne—this is Emperor Yao.
translations.literal: Emperor Zhi being established was not good; he died, and his younger brother Fangxun was established; this is Emperor Yao.

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ku took a Chenfeng woman to wife and she bore Fangxun. He also married a Zouzi woman and she bore Zhi. When Emperor Ku died, Zhi succeeded him. Zhi proved an unworthy ruler and soon died; his younger brother Fangxun took the throne—this is Emperor Yao.
translations.literal: Emperor Ku married a daughter of the Chenfeng clan and begot Fangxun. He married a daughter of the Zouzi clan and begot Zhi. Emperor Ku died and Zhi replaced him. Emperor Zhi being established was not good; he died, and his younger brother Fangxun was established; this is Emperor Yao.

sentences.zh: 帝堯者，放勛。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yao’s personal name was Fangxun.
translations.literal: Emperor Yao was Fangxun.

sentences.zh: 其仁如天，其知如神。

translations.idiomatic: His kindness embraced all like heaven itself; his wisdom pierced like a spirit’s.
translations.literal: His benevolence was like Heaven; his knowledge was like a spirit’s.

sentences.zh: 就之如日，望之如雲。

translations.idiomatic: To draw near him felt like standing in sunlight; to look on him was like beholding drifting clouds.
translations.literal: Approaching him was like the sun; gazing on him was like clouds.

sentences.zh: 富而不驕，貴而不舒。

translations.idiomatic: Though wealthy he showed no arrogance; though exalted he never grew careless.
translations.literal: Rich yet not proud, noble yet not slack.

sentences.zh: 黃收純衣，彤車乘白馬。

translations.idiomatic: He wore a dark robe with yellow turban bands and rode a vermilion chariot drawn by white horses.
translations.literal: Yellow hatbands and dark upper robes, red carriage, white horses.

sentences.zh: 能明馴德，以親九族。

translations.idiomatic: He could refine his virtue and draw the nine agnates into harmony.
translations.literal: Able to brighten and tame virtue, thereby drawing close the nine kindreds.

sentences.zh: 九族既睦，便章百姓。

translations.idiomatic: Once the nine branches of the clan were at peace, he brought light to every lineage.
translations.literal: The nine kindreds being harmonious, he then made brilliant the hundred surnames.

sentences.zh: 百姓昭明，合和萬國。

translations.idiomatic: When every house stood clear in duty, he knit the myriad states into concord.
translations.literal: The hundred surnames being brilliant, he united and harmonized the myriad states.

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yao’s personal name was Fangxun. His kindness embraced all like heaven itself; his wisdom pierced like a spirit’s. To draw near him felt like standing in sunlight; to look on him was like beholding drifting clouds. Though wealthy he showed no arrogance; though exalted he never grew careless. He wore a dark robe with yellow turban bands and rode a vermilion chariot drawn by white horses. He could refine his virtue and draw the nine agnates into harmony. Once the nine branches of the clan were at peace, he brought light to every lineage. When every house stood clear in duty, he knit the myriad states into concord.
translations.literal: Emperor Yao was Fangxun. His benevolence was like Heaven; his knowledge was like a spirit’s. Approaching him was like the sun; gazing on him was like clouds. Rich yet not proud, noble yet not slack. Yellow hatbands and dark upper robes, red carriage, white horses. Able to brighten and tame virtue, thereby drawing close the nine kindreds. The nine kindreds being harmonious, he then made brilliant the hundred surnames. The hundred surnames being brilliant, he united and harmonized the myriad states.

sentences.zh: 乃命羲、和，敬順昊天，數法日月星辰，敬授民時。

translations.idiomatic: He charged Xi and He to reverence High Heaven, to calculate the courses of sun, moon, and stars, and to teach the people the calendar in due season.
translations.literal: Thereupon he commanded Xi and He reverently to follow August Heaven, to number and model sun, moon, and stars, and reverently to hand down the seasons to the people.

sentences.zh: 分命羲仲，居郁夷，曰暘谷。

translations.idiomatic: He separately ordered Xi Zhong to reside at Yuanyi, the Bright Valley of the east.
translations.literal: Separately he commanded Xi Zhong to dwell at Yuanyi, called the Valley of Dawn.

sentences.zh: 敬道日出，便程東作。

translations.idiomatic: There Xi Zhong was to reverence the sun’s rising path and set the spring work of the eastern quarter.
translations.literal: Reverently to mark the road of the rising sun and to arrange the labors of spring in the east.

sentences.zh: 日中，星鳥，以殷中春。

translations.idiomatic: At noon when the Bird asterism culminated, they fixed mid-spring.
translations.literal: When the sun stood at midcourse, the asterism Bird marked mid-spring.

sentences.zh: 其民析，鳥獸字微。

translations.idiomatic: The people went abroad to their tasks; birds and beasts paired and grew soft new fur.
translations.literal: The people dispersed; birds and beasts bred and their coats grew fine.

sentences.zh: 申命羲叔，居南交。

translations.idiomatic: He next ordered Xi Shu to take post at Southern Intersection.
translations.literal: Separately he commanded Xi Shu to dwell at Southern Intersection.

sentences.zh: 便程南為，敬致。

translations.idiomatic: There Xi Shu arranged the southern tasks and reverently observed midsummer.
translations.literal: He arranged the labors of the south and reverently waited on the summer.

sentences.zh: 日永，星火，以正中夏。

translations.idiomatic: On the longest day, when the Fire asterism stood at zenith, they fixed midsummer.
translations.literal: The day longest, the asterism Fire marked mid-summer.

sentences.zh: 其民因，鳥獸希革。

translations.idiomatic: The people sought the heights; birds and beasts moulted their winter pelage.
translations.literal: The people dwelt high; birds and beasts shed their coarse coats.

sentences.zh: 申命和仲，居西土，曰昧谷。

translations.idiomatic: He then sent He Zhong west to the land called Gloom Valley.
translations.literal: Separately he commanded He Zhong to dwell in the western lands, called the Valley of Gloom.

sentences.zh: 敬道日入，便程西成。

translations.idiomatic: He Zhong reverenced the sun’s setting path and ordered the autumn labors of the western quarter.
translations.literal: Reverently to mark the road of the setting sun and to arrange the harvest labors of the west.

sentences.zh: 夜中，星虛，以正中秋。

translations.idiomatic: At midnight when the Void asterism crossed the meridian, they fixed mid-autumn.
translations.literal: Night at midpoint, the asterism Void marked mid-autumn.

sentences.zh: 其民夷易，鳥獸毛毨。

translations.idiomatic: The people grew calm; birds and beasts grew sleek new coats.
translations.literal: The people were level and easy; birds and beasts had sleek new down.

sentences.zh: 申命和叔；

translations.idiomatic: Finally he ordered He Shu.
translations.literal: Separately he commanded He Shu.

sentences.zh: 居北方，曰幽都。

translations.idiomatic: He Shu was to dwell in the north at Dark Capital.
translations.literal: To dwell in the north, called Dark Capital.

sentences.zh: 便在伏物。

translations.idiomatic: There he regulated the dormant creatures of winter.
translations.literal: To arrange the hiding things.

sentences.zh: 日短，星昴，以正中冬。

translations.idiomatic: On the shortest day, when Mao culminated, they fixed midwinter.
translations.literal: The day shortest, the asterism Mao marked mid-winter.

sentences.zh: 其民燠，鳥獸氄毛。

translations.idiomatic: The people kept to the warmth indoors; birds and beasts grew heavy winter down.
translations.literal: The people warmed themselves; birds and beasts grew thick down.

sentences.zh: 歲三百六十六日，以閏月正四時。

translations.idiomatic: The year was reckoned at three hundred sixty-six days, with intercalary months to true the four seasons.
translations.literal: The year has three hundred sixty-six days; by intercalary months the four seasons are corrected.

sentences.zh: 信飭百官，眾功皆興。

translations.idiomatic: He disciplined the hundred bureaus with strict faithfulness, and every undertaking throve.
translations.literal: Faithfully he corrected the hundred offices, and every task flourished.

translations.idiomatic: He charged Xi and He to reverence High Heaven, to calculate the courses of sun, moon, and stars, and to teach the people the calendar in due season. He separately ordered Xi Zhong to reside at Yuanyi, the Bright Valley of the east. There Xi Zhong was to reverence the sun’s rising path and set the spring work of the eastern quarter. At noon when the Bird asterism culminated, they fixed mid-spring. The people went abroad to their tasks; birds and beasts paired and grew soft new fur. He next ordered Xi Shu to take post at Southern Intersection. There Xi Shu arranged the southern tasks and reverently observed midsummer. On the longest day, when the Fire asterism stood at zenith, they fixed midsummer. The people sought the heights; birds and beasts moulted their winter pelage. He then sent He Zhong west to the land called Gloom Valley. He Zhong reverenced the sun’s setting path and ordered the autumn labors of the western quarter. At midnight when the Void asterism crossed the meridian, they fixed mid-autumn. The people grew calm; birds and beasts grew sleek new coats. Finally he ordered He Shu. He Shu was to dwell in the north at Dark Capital. There he regulated the dormant creatures of winter. On the shortest day, when Mao culminated, they fixed midwinter. The people kept to the warmth indoors; birds and beasts grew heavy winter down. The year was reckoned at three hundred sixty-six days, with intercalary months to true the four seasons. He disciplined the hundred bureaus with strict faithfulness, and every undertaking throve.
translations.literal: Thereupon he commanded Xi and He reverently to follow August Heaven, to number and model sun, moon, and stars, and reverently to hand down the seasons to the people. Separately he commanded Xi Zhong to dwell at Yuanyi, called the Valley of Dawn. Reverently to mark the road of the rising sun and to arrange the labors of spring in the east. The day shortest, the asterism Mao marked mid-winter. The people warmed themselves; birds and beasts grew thick down. The year has three hundred sixty-six days; by intercalary months the four seasons are corrected. Faithfully he corrected the hundred offices, and every task flourished.

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「誰可順此事？」

translations.idiomatic: Yao asked, “Who can be charged with these affairs?”
translations.literal: Yao said, “Who may be entrusted with this affair?”

sentences.zh: 放齊曰：「嗣子丹朱開明。」

translations.idiomatic: Fang Qi answered, “Your heir Dan Zhu is bright enough for the task.”
translations.literal: Fang Qi said, “The heir son Dan Zhu is enlightened.”

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「吁！

translations.idiomatic: Yao sighed, “Ah—
translations.literal: Yao said, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 頑凶，不用。」

translations.idiomatic: the boy is stubborn and depraved; he will not do.”
translations.literal: Stubborn and vicious—he cannot be used.”

sentences.zh: 堯又曰：「誰可者？」

translations.idiomatic: Yao asked again, “Who then is fit?”
translations.literal: Yao again said, “Who may do it?”

sentences.zh: 讙兜曰：「共工旁聚布功，可用。」

translations.idiomatic: Huan Dou proposed Gonggong: “He gathers merit on every side; appoint him.”
translations.literal: Huan Dou said, “Gonggong everywhere gathers and displays merit; he may be used.”

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「共工善言，其用僻，似恭漫天，不可。」

translations.idiomatic: Yao replied, “Gonggong talks fair but acts crooked; he feigns reverence while scorning heaven—he is unfit.”
translations.literal: Yao said, “Gonggong’s words are fair, but his deeds are crooked; he seems respectful yet he insults High Heaven—he cannot be used.”

sentences.zh: 堯又曰：「嗟，四嶽，湯湯洪水滔天，浩浩懷山襄陵，下民其憂，有能使治者？」

translations.idiomatic: Yao cried, “O Four Peaks, the deluge rolls to the very sky; it swallows hills and clasps ridges, and the people below lament—who can be sent to tame it?”
translations.literal: Yao again said, “Oh, you Four Peaks, the floodwaters surge to heaven; vast and wide they embrace hills and girdle heights; the lower people groan—who can be sent to govern them?”

sentences.zh: 皆曰鯀可。

translations.idiomatic: All cried, “Let Gun try.”
translations.literal: All said Gun might.

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「鯀負命毀族，不可。」

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “Gun defies orders and shames his clan; he will not do.”
translations.literal: Yao said, “Gun defies commands and ruins his kin; he cannot be used.”

sentences.zh: 嶽曰：「異哉，試不可用而已。」

translations.idiomatic: The Peaks urged, “At least try him—if he fails you may cast him aside.”
translations.literal: The Peaks said, “Different indeed—try him; if he fails, dismiss him.”

sentences.zh: 堯於是聽嶽用鯀。

translations.idiomatic: Yao yielded to the Peaks and gave Gun the work.
translations.literal: Yao thereupon listened to the Peaks and employed Gun.

sentences.zh: 九歲，功用不成。

translations.idiomatic: Nine years passed and Gun accomplished nothing.
translations.literal: Nine years, his task was not completed.

translations.idiomatic: Yao asked, “Who can be charged with these affairs?” Fang Qi answered, “Your heir Dan Zhu is bright enough for the task.” Yao sighed, “Ah— the boy is stubborn and depraved; he will not do.” Yao asked again, “Who then is fit?” Huan Dou proposed Gonggong: “He gathers merit on every side; appoint him.” Yao replied, “Gonggong talks fair but acts crooked; he feigns reverence while scorning heaven—he is unfit.” Yao cried, “O Four Peaks, the deluge rolls to the very sky; it swallows hills and clasps ridges, and the people below lament—who can be sent to tame it?” All cried, “Let Gun try.” Yao said, “Gun defies orders and shames his clan; he will not do.” The Peaks urged, “At least try him—if he fails you may cast him aside.” Yao yielded to the Peaks and gave Gun the work. Nine years passed and Gun accomplished nothing.
translations.literal: Yao said, “Who may be entrusted with this affair?” Fang Qi said, “The heir son Dan Zhu is enlightened.” Yao said, “Alas! Stubborn and vicious—he cannot be used.” Yao again said, “Who may do it?” Huan Dou said, “Gonggong everywhere gathers and displays merit; he may be used.” Yao said, “Gonggong’s words are fair, but his deeds are crooked; he seems respectful yet he insults High Heaven—he cannot be used.” Yao again said, “Oh, you Four Peaks, the floodwaters surge to heaven; vast and wide they embrace hills and girdle heights; the lower people groan—who can be sent to govern them?” All said Gun might. Yao said, “Gun defies commands and ruins his kin; he cannot be used.” The Peaks said, “Different indeed—try him; if he fails, dismiss him.” Yao thereupon listened to the Peaks and employed Gun. Nine years, his task was not completed.

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「嗟！

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “Ah!
translations.literal: Yao said, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 四嶽：朕在位七十載，汝能庸命，踐朕位？」

translations.idiomatic: O Four Peaks, seventy years I have held the throne—will any of you take my charge and mount my seat?”
translations.literal: You Four Peaks: I have sat on the throne seventy years—can you carry out my mandate and mount my place?”

sentences.zh: 嶽應曰：「鄙德忝帝位。」

translations.idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is too slight—we would disgrace the throne.”
translations.literal: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is mean; we shame the imperial seat.”

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「悉舉貴戚及疏遠隱匿者。」

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “Search out everyone, near kin or far, known or hidden.”
translations.literal: Yao said, “Raise altogether noble kin and distant hidden ones.”

sentences.zh: 眾皆言於堯曰：「有矜在民閒，曰虞舜。」

translations.idiomatic: The assembly told Yao, “Among the commoners there is a bachelor named Shun of Yu.”
translations.literal: The multitude all spoke to Yao, saying, “There is one unwed among the people, called Yu Shun.”

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「然，朕聞之。

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “I have heard of him.”
translations.literal: Yao said, “Indeed, I have heard of him.

sentences.zh: 其何如？」

translations.idiomatic: What sort of man is he?”
translations.literal: What of his person?”

sentences.zh: 嶽曰：「盲者子。

translations.idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “He is the son of a blind father.
translations.literal: The Peaks said, “He is a blind man’s son.

sentences.zh: 父頑，母嚚，弟傲，能和以孝，烝烝治，不至姦。」

translations.idiomatic: The father is brutish, the mother spiteful, the half-brother overbearing, yet Shun has made peace in the house through filial duty and brotherly love, so that no evil takes root.”
translations.literal: The father is stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother arrogant, yet he can harmonize them with filial piety; he lifts them to good order so that wickedness does not enter.”

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「吾其試哉。」

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “Then I shall test him.”
translations.literal: Yao said, “I will try him.”

sentences.zh: 於是堯妻之二女，觀其德於二女。

translations.idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to Shun to see how he treated them.
translations.literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his virtue toward the two women.

sentences.zh: 舜飭下二女於媯汭，如婦禮。

translations.idiomatic: Shun settled the princesses on the south bank of the Guirui, and they kept every rule of a wife’s conduct.
translations.literal: Shun lowered the two daughters to the bend of Guirui and they observed the rites of wives.

translations.idiomatic: Yao said, “Ah! O Four Peaks, seventy years I have held the throne—will any of you take my charge and mount my seat?” The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is too slight—we would disgrace the throne.” Yao said, “Search out everyone, near kin or far, known or hidden.” The assembly told Yao, “Among the commoners there is a bachelor named Shun of Yu.” Yao said, “I have heard of him.” What sort of man is he?” The Peaks answered, “He is the son of a blind father. The father is brutish, the mother spiteful, the half-brother overbearing, yet Shun has made peace in the house through filial duty and brotherly love, so that no evil takes root.” Yao said, “Then I shall test him.” Yao therefore married his two daughters to Shun to see how he treated them. Shun settled the princesses on the south bank of the Guirui, and they kept every rule of a wife’s conduct.
translations.literal: Yao said, “Alas! You Four Peaks: I have sat on the throne seventy years—can you carry out my mandate and mount my place?” The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is mean; we shame the imperial seat.” Yao said, “Raise altogether noble kin and distant hidden ones.” The multitude all spoke to Yao, saying, “There is one unwed among the people, called Yu Shun.” Yao said, “Indeed, I have heard of him. What of his person?” The Peaks said, “He is a blind man’s son. The father is stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother arrogant, yet he can harmonize them with filial piety; he lifts them to good order so that wickedness does not enter.” Yao said, “I will try him.” Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his virtue toward the two women. Shun lowered the two daughters to the bend of Guirui and they observed the rites of wives.

sentences.zh: 堯善之，乃使舜慎和五典，五典能從。

translations.idiomatic: Pleased with Shun, Yao set him to put the five relationships in order, and they fell into harmony.
translations.literal: Yao approved him and thereupon made Shun carefully harmonize the five norms; the five norms obeyed.

sentences.zh: 乃遍入百官，百官時序。

translations.idiomatic: He next sent him through every ministry, and each office kept its proper season.
translations.literal: He then everywhere entered the hundred offices; the hundred offices were timely in order.

sentences.zh: 賓於四門，四門穆穆，諸侯遠方賓客皆敬。

translations.idiomatic: He stationed him at the four outer gates; all was dignity, and lords from afar came as guests in awe.
translations.literal: He received guests at the four gates; the four gates were solemn, and feudal lords and distant guests all showed reverence.

sentences.zh: 堯使舜入山林川澤，暴風雷雨，舜行不迷。

translations.idiomatic: Yao sent him into hills and marshes in howling wind and driving rain; Shun never lost his way.
translations.literal: Yao sent Shun into the mountains and forests amid violent wind, thunder, and rain; Shun walked and was not lost.

sentences.zh: 堯以為聖，召舜曰：「女謀事至而言可績，三年矣。

translations.idiomatic: Seeing his sagacity, Yao called Shun and said, “Three years you have planned the work of state and every word has borne fruit.
translations.literal: Yao deemed him sage and summoned Shun, saying, “You plan affairs and your words can be carried out three years.

sentences.zh: 女登帝位。」

translations.idiomatic: Mount the throne.”
translations.literal: Ascend the imperial seat.”

sentences.zh: 舜讓於德不懌。

translations.idiomatic: Shun declined out of modesty and would not presume to accept.
translations.literal: Shun yielded on account of virtue and was not pleased.

sentences.zh: 正月上日，舜受終於文祖。

translations.idiomatic: On an auspicious day in the first month Shun received the great charge in Wenzu’s temple.
translations.literal: On the first auspicious day of the first month Shun received the abdication at Wen Temple.

sentences.zh: 文祖者，堯大祖也。

translations.idiomatic: Wenzu was the temple of Yao’s supreme ancestor.
translations.literal: Wen Temple is Yao’s great ancestor.

translations.idiomatic: Pleased with Shun, Yao set him to put the five relationships in order, and they fell into harmony. He next sent him through every ministry, and each office kept its proper season. He stationed him at the four outer gates; all was dignity, and lords from afar came as guests in awe. Yao sent him into hills and marshes in howling wind and driving rain; Shun never lost his way. Seeing his sagacity, Yao called Shun and said, “Three years you have planned the work of state and every word has borne fruit. Mount the throne.” Shun declined out of modesty and would not presume to accept. On an auspicious day in the first month Shun received the great charge in Wenzu’s temple. Wenzu was the temple of Yao’s supreme ancestor.
translations.literal: Yao approved him and thereupon made Shun carefully harmonize the five norms; the five norms obeyed. He then everywhere entered the hundred offices; the hundred offices were timely in order. He received guests at the four gates; the four gates were solemn, and feudal lords and distant guests all showed reverence. Yao sent Shun into the mountains and forests amid violent wind, thunder, and rain; Shun walked and was not lost. Yao deemed him sage and summoned Shun, saying, “You plan affairs and your words can be carried out three years. Ascend the imperial seat.” Shun yielded on account of virtue and was not pleased. On the first auspicious day of the first month Shun received the abdication at Wen Temple. Wen Temple is Yao’s great ancestor.

sentences.zh: 於是帝堯老，命舜攝行天子之政，以觀天命。

translations.idiomatic: Yao had grown old, so he ordered Shun to act as regent and govern as Son of Heaven while heaven’s favor was read.
translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Yao being aged, he commanded Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government in order to observe Heaven’s mandate.

sentences.zh: 舜乃在璿璣玉衡，以齊七政。

translations.idiomatic: Shun aligned the seven luminaries with the pearl-sphere and jade sight-tube.
translations.literal: Shun then used the pearl-adorned armillary and the jade traverse to align the seven regulators.

sentences.zh: 遂類于上帝，禋于六宗，望于山川，辯于群神。

translations.idiomatic: He ranked sacrifices to High Heaven, offered smoke to the six tutelary powers, looked toward the great mountains and streams, and sorted the lesser gods.
translations.literal: Then he classified offerings to High Heaven, performed the yin rite to the six honors, sacrificed to mountains and rivers, and distinguished the host of spirits.

sentences.zh: 揖五瑞，擇吉月日，見四嶽諸牧，班瑞。

translations.idiomatic: He assembled the five regalia, chose lucky days, convened the Four Peaks and regional lords, and handed out the tallies.
translations.literal: He gathered the five tokens, chose an auspicious moon and day, met the Four Peaks and the shepherds, and distributed the tokens.

sentences.zh: 歲二月，東巡狩，至於岱宗，祡，望秩於山川。

translations.idiomatic: Every second month he toured east to Tai Shan, kindled the great fire sacrifice, and offered to the peaks and rivers in due order.
translations.literal: In the second month of the year he toured east to Mount Dai, burned offerings, and sacrificed to mountains and rivers in their ranks.

sentences.zh: 遂見東方君長，合時月正日，同律度量衡，修五禮五玉三帛二生一死為摯，如五器，卒乃復。

translations.idiomatic: He then greeted the eastern chiefs, set calendar and seasons in accord, unified pitch-pipes, bushels, and balances, polished the five courtesies and the jade tokens, silks, and guest-gifts of live animals; when the rites ended he restored the regalia.
translations.literal: Then he met the eastern lords, harmonized seasons, months, and days, unified pitches, measures, weights, and balances, refined the five rites, the five jades, the three silks, the two live and one dead gifts for guests; as with the five regalia, when finished he returned them.

sentences.zh: 五月，南巡狩；

translations.idiomatic: In the fifth month he toured the south.
translations.literal: In the fifth month he toured south.

sentences.zh: 八月，西巡狩；

translations.idiomatic: In the eighth month he toured the west.
translations.literal: In the eighth month he toured west.

sentences.zh: 十一月，北巡狩：皆如初。

translations.idiomatic: In the eleventh month he toured the north—each circuit followed the same pattern.
translations.literal: In the eleventh month he toured north: all as at first.

sentences.zh: 歸，至于祖禰廟，用特牛禮。

translations.idiomatic: On his return he reached the shrines of founder and distant ancestors and sacrificed a bull with the highest rite.
translations.literal: Returning, he reached the temple of the founding and distant ancestors and offered a bull with the special rite.

sentences.zh: 五歲一巡狩，群后四朝。

translations.idiomatic: He toured the realm once in five years, and the lords presented themselves at court on the four years between.
translations.literal: Every five years one tour; the hosts of lords attended court four times between tours.

sentences.zh: 遍告以言，明試以功，車服以庸。

translations.idiomatic: He proclaimed his orders in speech, tested officers by their deeds, and rewarded merit with carriages and robes.
translations.literal: Everywhere he announced commands with words, clearly tested merit with deeds, and with chariots and robes rewarded achievement.

sentences.zh: 肇十有二州，決川。

translations.idiomatic: He first carved the realm into twelve provinces and dredged their streams.
translations.literal: He began the division into twelve provinces and opened the rivers.

sentences.zh: 象以典刑，流宥五刑，鞭作官刑，撲作教刑，金作贖刑。

translations.idiomatic: He took the standing statutes as models for punishments, used exile to commute the five corporal pains, appointed the lash for official faults, the rod for moral instruction, and fines of metal for redeemable crimes.
translations.literal: He patterned punishments on the model statutes, by ban pardoned the five punishments, with the whip made office punishments, with the rod made instructional punishments, with metal made ransom punishments.

sentences.zh: 眚災過，赦；

translations.idiomatic: Accidents and first faults he forgave.
translations.literal: For oversight calamities and oversights he pardoned.

sentences.zh: 怙終賊，刑。

translations.idiomatic: Habitual evil he punished without mercy.
translations.literal: For those who stubbornly ended in villainy he applied punishment.

sentences.zh: 欽哉，欽哉，惟刑之靜哉！

translations.idiomatic: Be vigilant, be reverent—see that justice stays measured and calm.”
translations.literal: Revere, reverence—only let punishments be quiet.”

translations.idiomatic: Yao had grown old, so he ordered Shun to act as regent and govern as Son of Heaven while heaven’s favor was read. Shun aligned the seven luminaries with the pearl-sphere and jade sight-tube. He ranked sacrifices to High Heaven, offered smoke to the six tutelary powers, looked toward the great mountains and streams, and sorted the lesser gods. He assembled the five regalia, chose lucky days, convened the Four Peaks and regional lords, and handed out the tallies. Every second month he toured east to Tai Shan, kindled the great fire sacrifice, and offered to the peaks and rivers in due order. He then greeted the eastern chiefs, set calendar and seasons in accord, unified pitch-pipes, bushels, and balances, polished the five courtesies and the jade tokens, silks, and guest-gifts of live animals; when the rites ended he restored the regalia. In the fifth month he toured the south. In the eighth month he toured the west. In the eleventh month he toured the north—each circuit followed the same pattern. On his return he reached the shrines of founder and distant ancestors and sacrificed a bull with the highest rite. He toured the realm once in five years, and the lords presented themselves at court on the four years between. He proclaimed his orders in speech, tested officers by their deeds, and rewarded merit with carriages and robes. He first carved the realm into twelve provinces and dredged their streams. He took the standing statutes as models for punishments, used exile to commute the five corporal pains, appointed the lash for official faults, the rod for moral instruction, and fines of metal for redeemable crimes. Accidents and first faults he forgave. Habitual evil he punished without mercy. Be vigilant, be reverent—see that justice stays measured and calm.”
translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Yao being aged, he commanded Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government in order to observe Heaven’s mandate. Shun then used the pearl-adorned armillary and the jade traverse to align the seven regulators. Then he classified offerings to High Heaven, performed the yin rite to the six honors, sacrificed to mountains and rivers, and distinguished the host of spirits. He gathered the five tokens, chose an auspicious moon and day, met the Four Peaks and the shepherds, and distributed the tokens. In the second month of the year he toured east to Mount Dai, burned offerings, and sacrificed to mountains and rivers in their ranks. Then he met the eastern lords, harmonized seasons, months, and days, unified pitches, measures, weights, and balances, refined the five rites, the five jades, the three silks, the two live and one dead gifts for guests; as with the five regalia, when finished he returned them. In the fifth month he toured south. In the eighth month he toured west. In the eleventh month he toured north: all as at first. Returning, he reached the temple of the founding and distant ancestors and offered a bull with the special rite. Every five years one tour; the hosts of lords attended court four times between tours. Everywhere he announced commands with words, clearly tested merit with deeds, and with chariots and robes rewarded achievement. He began the division into twelve provinces and opened the rivers. He patterned punishments on the model statutes, by ban pardoned the five punishments, with the whip made office punishments, with the rod made instructional punishments, with metal made ransom punishments. For oversight calamities and oversights he pardoned. For those who stubbornly ended in villainy he applied punishment. Revere, reverence—only let punishments be quiet.”

sentences.zh: 讙兜進言共工，堯曰不可而試之工師，共工果淫辟。

translations.idiomatic: Huan Dou praised Gonggong; Yao refused the post but still tested him as minister of works, and Gonggong proved wanton and corrupt.
translations.literal: Huan Dou spoke well of Gonggong; Yao said he could not be used, yet tried him as master of works, and Gonggong indeed ran to excess.

sentences.zh: 四嶽舉鯀治鴻水，堯以為不可，嶽彊請試之，試之而無功，故百姓不便。

translations.idiomatic: The Four Peaks urged Gun to master the flood; Yao doubted him, yet at their insistence gave him trial; Gun failed, and the people suffered for it.
translations.literal: The Four Peaks raised Gun to govern the vast flood; Yao deemed it impossible, but the Peaks urgently begged to try him; having tried him he had no success, so the hundred surnames found it inconvenient.

sentences.zh: 三苗在江淮、荊州數為亂。

translations.idiomatic: The Three Miao tribes along the Yangzi, Han, and Jingzhou rose again and again.
translations.literal: The Three Miao in the Yangzi and Han region and Jingzhou repeatedly made rebellion.

sentences.zh: 於是舜歸而言於帝，請流共工於幽陵，以變北狄；

translations.idiomatic: Shun then reported to Yao and asked to exile Gonggong to Youling to chasten the northern Di.
translations.literal: Thereupon Shun returned and spoke to the emperor, asking to banish Gonggong to Youling to change the northern Di.

sentences.zh: 放讙兜於崇山，以變南蠻；

translations.idiomatic: He banished Huan Dou to Chongshan to awe the southern Man.
translations.literal: To banish Huan Dou to Chongshan to change the southern Man.

sentences.zh: 遷三苗於三危，以變西戎；

translations.idiomatic: He removed the Three Miao to Sanwei to humble the western Rong.
translations.literal: To move the Three Miao to Sanwei to change the western Rong.

sentences.zh: 殛鯀於羽山，以變東夷：四罪而天下咸服。

translations.idiomatic: He slew Gun on Mount Yu’s slopes to warn the eastern Yi—with these four punishments the realm submitted.
translations.literal: To execute Gun on Mount Yu to change the eastern Yi: with these four crimes all under Heaven submitted.

translations.idiomatic: Huan Dou praised Gonggong; Yao refused the post but still tested him as minister of works, and Gonggong proved wanton and corrupt. The Four Peaks urged Gun to master the flood; Yao doubted him, yet at their insistence gave him trial; Gun failed, and the people suffered for it. The Three Miao tribes along the Yangzi, Han, and Jingzhou rose again and again. Shun then reported to Yao and asked to exile Gonggong to Youling to chasten the northern Di. He banished Huan Dou to Chongshan to awe the southern Man. He removed the Three Miao to Sanwei to humble the western Rong. He slew Gun on Mount Yu’s slopes to warn the eastern Yi—with these four punishments the realm submitted.
translations.literal: Huan Dou spoke well of Gonggong; Yao said he could not be used, yet tried him as master of works, and Gonggong indeed ran to excess. The Four Peaks raised Gun to govern the vast flood; Yao deemed it impossible, but the Peaks urgently begged to try him; having tried him he had no success, so the hundred surnames found it inconvenient. The Three Miao in the Yangzi and Han region and Jingzhou repeatedly made rebellion. Thereupon Shun returned and spoke to the emperor, asking to banish Gonggong to Youling to change the northern Di. To banish Huan Dou to Chongshan to change the southern Man. To move the Three Miao to Sanwei to change the western Rong. To execute Gun on Mount Yu to change the eastern Yi: with these four crimes all under Heaven submitted.

sentences.zh: 堯立七十年得舜，二十年而老，令舜攝行天子之政，薦之於天。

translations.idiomatic: Seventy years Yao reigned before he found Shun; twenty years later, enfeebled, he made Shun regent and commended him to heaven.
translations.literal: Yao seventy years on the throne obtained Shun; twenty years more he grew old and ordered Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and presented him to Heaven.

sentences.zh: 堯辟位凡二十八年而崩。

translations.idiomatic: Twenty-eight years after yielding the throne Yao died.
translations.literal: Yao yielded the seat twenty-eight years altogether and then died.

sentences.zh: 百姓悲哀，如喪父母。

translations.idiomatic: The people mourned as though they had lost their own parents.
translations.literal: The hundred surnames were sorrowful as if mourning father and mother.

sentences.zh: 三年，四方莫舉樂，以思堯。

translations.idiomatic: For three years no music sounded within the four seas, so deep was the grief for Yao.
translations.literal: For three years within the four quarters none raised music, thinking on Yao.

sentences.zh: 堯知子丹朱之不肖，不足授天下，於是乃權授舜。

translations.idiomatic: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was unworthy and could not receive the realm, so he weighed the matter and transferred the mandate to Shun.
translations.literal: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was not worthy and insufficient to receive all under Heaven; therefore he weighed and handed the realm to Shun.

sentences.zh: 授舜，則天下得其利而丹朱病；

translations.idiomatic: To give the throne to Shun would bless the world yet wound Dan Zhu alone;
translations.literal: Had he given it to Shun, all under Heaven would have gained the benefit and Dan Zhu alone would have suffered;

sentences.zh: 授丹朱，則天下病而丹朱得其利。

translations.idiomatic: to give it to Dan Zhu would torment the world while only Dan Zhu profited.
translations.literal: had he given it to Dan Zhu, all under Heaven would have suffered and Dan Zhu alone would have gained the benefit.

sentences.zh: 堯曰：「終不以天下之病而利一人」，而卒授舜以天下。

translations.idiomatic: Yao declared, “I will never harm the myriad people for one man’s sake,” and in the end he ceded the empire to Shun.
translations.literal: Yao said, “In the end I cannot cause all under Heaven to suffer for the sake of one man,” and finally he gave all under Heaven to Shun.

sentences.zh: 堯崩，三年之喪畢，舜讓辟丹朱於南河之南。

translations.idiomatic: After Yao’s death and the three years’ mourning, Shun yielded the throne to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.
translations.literal: When Yao died and the three years’ mourning were finished, Shun yielded to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.

sentences.zh: 諸侯朝覲者不之丹朱而之舜，獄訟者不之丹朱而之舜，謳歌者不謳歌丹朱而謳歌舜。

translations.idiomatic: Lords who attended court went to Shun, not Dan Zhu; plaintiffs sought Shun, not Dan Zhu; ballad-makers praised Shun, not Dan Zhu.
translations.literal: The feudal lords who came to court did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; litigants did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; singers did not sing of Dan Zhu but of Shun.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「天也」，夫而後之中國踐天子位焉，是為帝舜。

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “This is heaven’s choice,” entered the central plain, and took the throne—Emperor Shun.
translations.literal: Shun said, “It is Heaven,” and afterward entered the central domain and mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat; this is Emperor Shun.

translations.idiomatic: Seventy years Yao reigned before he found Shun; twenty years later, enfeebled, he made Shun regent and commended him to heaven. Twenty-eight years after yielding the throne Yao died. The people mourned as though they had lost their own parents. For three years no music sounded within the four seas, so deep was the grief for Yao. Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was unworthy and could not receive the realm, so he weighed the matter and transferred the mandate to Shun. To give the throne to Shun would bless the world yet wound Dan Zhu alone; to give it to Dan Zhu would torment the world while only Dan Zhu profited. Yao declared, “I will never harm the myriad people for one man’s sake,” and in the end he ceded the empire to Shun. After Yao’s death and the three years’ mourning, Shun yielded the throne to Dan Zhu south of the southern river. Lords who attended court went to Shun, not Dan Zhu; plaintiffs sought Shun, not Dan Zhu; ballad-makers praised Shun, not Dan Zhu. Shun said, “This is heaven’s choice,” entered the central plain, and took the throne—Emperor Shun.
translations.literal: Yao seventy years on the throne obtained Shun; twenty years more he grew old and ordered Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and presented him to Heaven. Yao yielded the seat twenty-eight years altogether and then died. The hundred surnames were sorrowful as if mourning father and mother. For three years within the four quarters none raised music, thinking on Yao. Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was not worthy and insufficient to receive all under Heaven; therefore he weighed and handed the realm to Shun. Had he given it to Shun, all under Heaven would have gained the benefit and Dan Zhu alone would have suffered; had he given it to Dan Zhu, all under Heaven would have suffered and Dan Zhu alone would have gained the benefit. Yao said, “In the end I cannot cause all under Heaven to suffer for the sake of one man,” and finally he gave all under Heaven to Shun. When Yao died and the three years’ mourning were finished, Shun yielded to Dan Zhu south of the southern river. The feudal lords who came to court did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; litigants did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; singers did not sing of Dan Zhu but of Shun. Shun said, “It is Heaven,” and afterward entered the central domain and mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat; this is Emperor Shun.

sentences.zh: 虞舜者，名曰重華。

translations.idiomatic: Shun of Yu was styled Chonghua.
translations.literal: Yu Shun was named Chonghua.

sentences.zh: 重華父曰瞽叟，瞽叟父曰橋牛，橋牛父曰句望，句望父曰敬康，敬康父曰窮蟬，窮蟬父曰帝顓頊，顓頊父曰昌意：以至舜七世矣。

translations.idiomatic: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou, whose father was Qiaoniu, whose father was Gouwang, whose father was Jingkang, whose father was Qiongchan, whose father was Emperor Zhuanxu, whose father was Changyi—seven generations from Shun to Zhuanxu.
translations.literal: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou; Gu Sou’s father was Qiaoniu; Qiaoniu’s father was Gouwang; Gouwang’s father was Jingkang; Jingkang’s father was Qiongchan; Qiongchan’s father was Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu’s father was Changyi: thus to Shun seven generations.

sentences.zh: 自從窮蟬以至帝舜，皆微為庶人。

translations.idiomatic: From Qiongchan down to Emperor Shun the line had sunk to commoners.
translations.literal: From Qiongchan to Emperor Shun all were humble commoners.

translations.idiomatic: Shun of Yu was styled Chonghua. Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou, whose father was Qiaoniu, whose father was Gouwang, whose father was Jingkang, whose father was Qiongchan, whose father was Emperor Zhuanxu, whose father was Changyi—seven generations from Shun to Zhuanxu. From Qiongchan down to Emperor Shun the line had sunk to commoners.
translations.literal: Yu Shun was named Chonghua. Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou; Gu Sou’s father was Qiaoniu; Qiaoniu’s father was Gouwang; Gouwang’s father was Jingkang; Jingkang’s father was Qiongchan; Qiongchan’s father was Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu’s father was Changyi: thus to Shun seven generations. From Qiongchan to Emperor Shun all were humble commoners.

sentences.zh: 舜父瞽叟盲，而舜母死，瞽叟更娶妻而生象，象傲。

translations.idiomatic: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother had died; Gu Sou remarried and fathered Xiang, a proud and overbearing son.
translations.literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother died; Gu Sou remarried and begot Xiang, who was arrogant.

sentences.zh: 瞽叟愛後妻子，常欲殺舜，舜避逃；

translations.idiomatic: Gu Sou doted on the second wife’s child and often plotted Shun’s death; Shun slipped away.
translations.literal: Gu Sou loved the later wife’s son and always wished to kill Shun; Shun fled and hid.

sentences.zh: 及有小過，則受罪。

translations.idiomatic: When charged with petty faults he bore the blame.
translations.literal: When he had small faults he received punishment.

sentences.zh: 順事父及後母與弟，日以篤謹，匪有解。

translations.idiomatic: Yet he served father, stepmother, and brother with growing devotion and never slackened.
translations.literal: With reverence he served father, stepmother, and younger brother; daily he grew more earnest and never slackened.

translations.idiomatic: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother had died; Gu Sou remarried and fathered Xiang, a proud and overbearing son. Gu Sou doted on the second wife’s child and often plotted Shun’s death; Shun slipped away. When charged with petty faults he bore the blame. Yet he served father, stepmother, and brother with growing devotion and never slackened.
translations.literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother died; Gu Sou remarried and begot Xiang, who was arrogant. Gu Sou loved the later wife’s son and always wished to kill Shun; Shun fled and hid. When he had small faults he received punishment. With reverence he served father, stepmother, and younger brother; daily he grew more earnest and never slackened.

sentences.zh: 舜，冀州之人也。

translations.idiomatic: Shun came from the Ji region.
translations.literal: Shun was a man of Jizhou.

sentences.zh: 舜耕歷山，漁雷澤，陶河濱，作什器於壽丘，就時於負夏。

translations.idiomatic: He ploughed Mount Li, fished Thunder Marsh, threw pots along the Yellow River, forged tools at Shouqiu, and hired out his labor at Fuxia.
translations.literal: Shun ploughed on Mount Li, fished in Thunder Marsh, potted by the Yellow River, fashioned tools at Shouqiu, and sought a livelihood at Fuxia.

sentences.zh: 舜父瞽叟頑，母嚚，弟象傲，皆欲殺舜。

translations.idiomatic: His father remained brutish, his stepmother spiteful, his brother Xiang arrogant; all three still schemed against his life.
translations.literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother Xiang arrogant; all wished to kill Shun.

sentences.zh: 舜順適不失子道，兄弟孝慈。

translations.idiomatic: Shun stayed filial and brotherly and never broke a son’s duty.
translations.literal: Shun obeyed and fitted himself and did not lose the way of a son; he was fraternal and filial.

sentences.zh: 欲殺，不可得；

translations.idiomatic: When they tried to kill him they could never catch him.
translations.literal: When they wished to kill him they could not get him.

sentences.zh: 即求，嘗在側。

translations.idiomatic: Whenever they looked for him he was still at hand to serve.
translations.literal: When they sought him he was always at their side.

translations.idiomatic: Shun came from the Ji region. He ploughed Mount Li, fished Thunder Marsh, threw pots along the Yellow River, forged tools at Shouqiu, and hired out his labor at Fuxia. His father remained brutish, his stepmother spiteful, his brother Xiang arrogant; all three still schemed against his life. Shun stayed filial and brotherly and never broke a son’s duty. When they tried to kill him they could never catch him. Whenever they looked for him he was still at hand to serve.
translations.literal: Shun was a man of Jizhou. Shun ploughed on Mount Li, fished in Thunder Marsh, potted by the Yellow River, fashioned tools at Shouqiu, and sought a livelihood at Fuxia. Shun’s father Gu Sou was stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother Xiang arrogant; all wished to kill Shun. Shun obeyed and fitted himself and did not lose the way of a son; he was fraternal and filial. When they wished to kill him they could not get him. When they sought him he was always at their side.

sentences.zh: 舜年二十以孝聞。

translations.idiomatic: At twenty his filial name was known everywhere.
translations.literal: At twenty Shun was famed for filial piety.

sentences.zh: 三十而帝堯問可用者，四嶽咸薦虞舜，曰可。

translations.idiomatic: At thirty, when Yao asked for worthy men, the Four Peaks together named Shun of Yu.
translations.literal: At thirty Emperor Yao asked who could be employed; the Four Peaks all recommended Yu Shun, saying he would do.

sentences.zh: 於是堯乃以二女妻舜以觀其內，使九男與處以觀其外。

translations.idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to watch Shun’s inner life and sent nine sons to live with him and watch his outer conduct.
translations.literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his conduct within, and sent nine sons to dwell with him to observe his conduct without.

sentences.zh: 舜居媯汭，內行彌謹。

translations.idiomatic: Shun lived on the Guirui bend and daily refined his private virtue.
translations.literal: Shun dwelt at the bend of Guirui; his inner conduct grew daily more careful.

sentences.zh: 堯二女不敢以貴驕事舜親戚，甚有婦道。

translations.idiomatic: The two princesses never flaunted their rank over Shun’s family but kept every wifely duty.
translations.literal: Yao’s two daughters did not dare to rely on their nobility to slight Shun’s kin; they were very observant of a wife’s way.

sentences.zh: 堯九男皆益篤。

translations.idiomatic: Yao’s nine sons grew steadier under Shun’s influence.
translations.literal: Yao’s nine sons all grew more honest.

sentences.zh: 舜耕歷山，歷山之人皆讓畔；

translations.idiomatic: When he ploughed Mount Li, the hill-folk yielded him the wider furrows.
translations.literal: When Shun ploughed Mount Li, the men of Mount Li yielded him the boundaries.

sentences.zh: 漁雷澤，雷澤上人皆讓居；

translations.idiomatic: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the shore-dwellers gave him the better stands.
translations.literal: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the men on Thunder Marsh yielded him dwelling sites.

sentences.zh: 陶河濱，河濱器皆不苦窳。

translations.idiomatic: When he threw pots on the Yellow River, no potter along the bank turned out cracked ware.
translations.literal: When he potted by the Yellow River, the vessels by the river were without flaw.

sentences.zh: 一年而所居成聚，二年成邑，三年成都。

translations.idiomatic: Within a year his hamlet swelled to a village, in two to a town, in three to a city.
translations.literal: In one year the place he dwelt became a village; in two years a town; in three years a capital.

sentences.zh: 堯乃賜舜絺衣，與琴，為筑倉廩，予牛羊。

translations.idiomatic: Yao then gifted him fine cloth and a zither, built granaries for him, and presented oxen and sheep.
translations.literal: Yao thereupon gave Shun fine linen, a zither, built him granaries, and gave him cattle and sheep.

sentences.zh: 瞽叟尚復欲殺之，使舜上涂廩，瞽叟從下縱火焚廩。

translations.idiomatic: Gu Sou still plotted murder: he sent Shun to roof the high granary, then fired the stacks from below.
translations.literal: Gu Sou still again wished to kill him; he made Shun mount the high granary while Gu Sou from below set fire to the granary.

sentences.zh: 舜乃以兩笠自捍而下，去，得不死。

translations.idiomatic: Shun opened two wide bamboo hats like wings and floated down, escaping the flames.
translations.literal: Shun then used two bamboo hats to shield himself and came down, went away, and did not die.

sentences.zh: 後瞽叟又使舜穿井，舜穿井為匿空旁出。

translations.idiomatic: Later Gu Sou ordered him to dig a well; Shun cut a secret lateral tunnel and climbed out.
translations.literal: Afterward Gu Sou again made Shun dig a well; Shun dug the well and made a hidden side tunnel to come out.

sentences.zh: 舜既入深，瞽叟與象共下土實井，舜從匿空出，去。

translations.idiomatic: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang dumped earth down the shaft; Shun slipped through the side passage and vanished.
translations.literal: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang together filled the well with earth; Shun came out through the hidden tunnel and went away.

sentences.zh: 瞽叟、象喜，以舜為已死。

translations.idiomatic: Father and brother rejoiced, sure Shun was dead.
translations.literal: Gu Sou and Xiang rejoiced, taking Shun for already dead.

sentences.zh: 象曰：「本謀者象。」

translations.idiomatic: Xiang said, “The scheme was my idea.”
translations.literal: Xiang said, “The plot was mine.”

sentences.zh: 象與其父母分，於是曰：「舜妻堯二女，與琴，象取之。

translations.idiomatic: Xiang divided the booty with his parents, saying, “Shun’s wives, Yao’s daughters, and his zither belong to me.
translations.literal: Xiang with his father and mother divided the spoils, and then said, “Shun’s wives—Yao’s two daughters—and the zither are mine.

sentences.zh: 牛羊倉廩予父母。」

translations.idiomatic: The herds and granaries go to you.”
translations.literal: The cattle, sheep, and granaries I give to father and mother.”

sentences.zh: 象乃止舜宮居，鼓其琴。

translations.idiomatic: Xiang marched into Shun’s quarters, took the zither, and played.
translations.literal: Xiang then entered Shun’s palace and dwelt there, strumming his zither.

sentences.zh: 舜往見之。

translations.idiomatic: Shun walked in on him.
translations.literal: Shun went in and saw him.

sentences.zh: 象鄂不懌，曰：「我思舜正郁陶！」

translations.idiomatic: Xiang jolted, sullen, said, “I was brooding on you, elder brother, and strumming out my sorrow.”
translations.literal: Xiang startled was not pleased and said, “I was thinking of you, brother, and was depressed.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「然，爾其庶矣！」

translations.idiomatic: Shun answered gently, “Yes, you are still my brother.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Yes, you are indeed my younger brother.”

sentences.zh: 舜復事瞽叟愛弟彌謹。

translations.idiomatic: Shun afterward served his father and cherished his brother with even greater care.
