translations.literal: Shun again served Gu Sou, loved his younger brother, and grew daily more careful.

sentences.zh: 於是堯乃試舜五典百官，皆治。

translations.idiomatic: Yao then tested Shun with the five relationships and every bureau—everything thrived.
translations.literal: Thereupon Yao tried Shun with the five norms and the hundred offices; all were governed.

translations.idiomatic: At twenty his filial name was known everywhere. At thirty, when Yao asked for worthy men, the Four Peaks together named Shun of Yu. Yao therefore married his two daughters to watch Shun’s inner life and sent nine sons to live with him and watch his outer conduct. Shun lived on the Guirui bend and daily refined his private virtue. The two princesses never flaunted their rank over Shun’s family but kept every wifely duty. Yao’s nine sons grew steadier under Shun’s influence. When he ploughed Mount Li, the hill-folk yielded him the wider furrows. When he fished Thunder Marsh, the shore-dwellers gave him the better stands. When he threw pots on the Yellow River, no potter along the bank turned out cracked ware. Within a year his hamlet swelled to a village, in two to a town, in three to a city. Yao then gifted him fine cloth and a zither, built granaries for him, and presented oxen and sheep. Gu Sou still plotted murder: he sent Shun to roof the high granary, then fired the stacks from below. Shun opened two wide bamboo hats like wings and floated down, escaping the flames. Later Gu Sou ordered him to dig a well; Shun cut a secret lateral tunnel and climbed out. When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang dumped earth down the shaft; Shun slipped through the side passage and vanished. Father and brother rejoiced, sure Shun was dead. Xiang said, “The scheme was my idea.” Xiang divided the booty with his parents, saying, “Shun’s wives, Yao’s daughters, and his zither belong to me. The herds and granaries go to you.” Xiang marched into Shun’s quarters, took the zither, and played. Shun walked in on him. Xiang jolted, sullen, said, “I was brooding on you, elder brother, and strumming out my sorrow.” Shun answered gently, “Yes, you are still my brother.” Shun afterward served his father and cherished his brother with even greater care. Yao then tested Shun with the five relationships and every bureau—everything thrived.
translations.literal: At twenty Shun was famed for filial piety. At thirty Emperor Yao asked who could be employed; the Four Peaks all recommended Yu Shun, saying he would do. Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his conduct within, and sent nine sons to dwell with him to observe his conduct without. Shun dwelt at the bend of Guirui; his inner conduct grew daily more careful. Yao’s two daughters did not dare to rely on their nobility to slight Shun’s kin; they were very observant of a wife’s way. Yao’s nine sons all grew more honest. When Shun ploughed Mount Li, the men of Mount Li yielded him the boundaries. When he fished Thunder Marsh, the men on Thunder Marsh yielded him dwelling sites. When he potted by the Yellow River, the vessels by the river were without flaw. In one year the place he dwelt became a village; in two years a town; in three years a capital. Yao thereupon gave Shun fine linen, a zither, built him granaries, and gave him cattle and sheep. Gu Sou still again wished to kill him; he made Shun mount the high granary while Gu Sou from below set fire to the granary. Shun then used two bamboo hats to shield himself and came down, went away, and did not die. Afterward Gu Sou again made Shun dig a well; Shun dug the well and made a hidden side tunnel to come out. When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang together filled the well with earth; Shun came out through the hidden tunnel and went away. Gu Sou and Xiang rejoiced, taking Shun for already dead. Xiang said, “The plot was mine.” Xiang with his father and mother divided the spoils, and then said, “Shun’s wives—Yao’s two daughters—and the zither are mine. The cattle, sheep, and granaries I give to father and mother.” Xiang then entered Shun’s palace and dwelt there, strumming his zither. Shun went in and saw him. Xiang startled was not pleased and said, “I was thinking of you, brother, and was depressed.” Shun said, “Yes, you are indeed my younger brother.” Shun again served Gu Sou, loved his younger brother, and grew daily more careful. Thereupon Yao tried Shun with the five norms and the hundred offices; all were governed.

sentences.zh: 昔高陽氏有才子八人，世得其利，謂之「八愷」。

translations.idiomatic: The Gaoyang line once had eight brilliant sons whose gifts blessed the age—the “Eight Harmonious.”
translations.literal: Formerly the Gaoyang clan had eight talented sons; the world gained their benefit and called them the Eight Harmonious.

sentences.zh: 高辛氏有才子八人，世謂之「八元」。

translations.idiomatic: The Gaoxin line had eight more paragons whom men called the “Eight Pures.”
translations.literal: The Gaoxin clan had eight talented sons; the world called them the Eight Pures.

sentences.zh: 此十六族者，世濟其美，不隕其名。

translations.idiomatic: These sixteen houses handed down their virtue and never stained their fame.
translations.literal: These sixteen families generation after generation completed their excellence and did not let their names fall.

sentences.zh: 至於堯，堯未能舉。

translations.idiomatic: Yet even under Yao they had not been promoted.
translations.literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not raise them.

sentences.zh: 舜舉八愷，使主后土，以揆百事，莫不時序。

translations.idiomatic: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and set them over the land to pace the hundred labors—nothing fell out of season.
translations.literal: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and made them oversee the earth to measure the hundred tasks; nothing missed its season.

sentences.zh: 舉八元，使布五教于四方，父義，母慈，兄友，弟恭，子孝，內平外成。

translations.idiomatic: He raised the Eight Pures to spread the five teachings abroad—fathers just, mothers tender, elder brothers kind, younger brothers deferential, sons filial—so that home was calm and the realm perfected.
translations.literal: He raised the Eight Pures and sent them to spread the five teachings through the four quarters: father righteous, mother kind, elder brother friendly, younger brother respectful, son filial; within there was peace, without there was completion.

translations.idiomatic: The Gaoyang line once had eight brilliant sons whose gifts blessed the age—the “Eight Harmonious.” The Gaoxin line had eight more paragons whom men called the “Eight Pures.” These sixteen houses handed down their virtue and never stained their fame. Yet even under Yao they had not been promoted. Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and set them over the land to pace the hundred labors—nothing fell out of season. He raised the Eight Pures to spread the five teachings abroad—fathers just, mothers tender, elder brothers kind, younger brothers deferential, sons filial—so that home was calm and the realm perfected.
translations.literal: Formerly the Gaoyang clan had eight talented sons; the world gained their benefit and called them the Eight Harmonious. The Gaoxin clan had eight talented sons; the world called them the Eight Pures. These sixteen families generation after generation completed their excellence and did not let their names fall. Down to Yao, Yao could not raise them. Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and made them oversee the earth to measure the hundred tasks; nothing missed its season. He raised the Eight Pures and sent them to spread the five teachings through the four quarters: father righteous, mother kind, elder brother friendly, younger brother respectful, son filial; within there was peace, without there was completion.

sentences.zh: 昔帝鴻氏有不才子，掩義隱賊，好行凶慝，天下謂之渾沌。

translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Hong clan bred a villain who smothered right and nursed evil, delighting in cruelty—men named him Chaos.
translations.literal: Formerly the Emperor Hong clan had an unworthy son who hid righteousness and harbored villainy, loving to do violence; the world called him Chaos.

sentences.zh: 少暤氏有不才子，毀信惡忠，崇飾惡言，天下謂之窮奇。

translations.idiomatic: The Shaohao line had a rogue who broke faith and spurned loyalty, trumpeting slander—men called him Wretch.
translations.literal: The Shaohao clan had an unworthy son who destroyed trust and hated loyalty, glorifying evil speech; the world called him Wretch.

sentences.zh: 顓頊氏有不才子，不可教訓，不知話言，天下謂之梼杌。

translations.idiomatic: The Zhuanxu clan produced a brute who would not learn or heed counsel—men called him Blockhead.
translations.literal: The Zhuanxu clan had an unworthy son who could not be taught or instructed, who did not understand good words; the world called him Blockhead.

sentences.zh: 此三族世憂之。

translations.idiomatic: For generations these three houses were the scourge of the age.
translations.literal: These three families generation after generation were the world’s sorrow.

sentences.zh: 至于堯，堯未能去。

translations.idiomatic: Even Yao could not uproot them.
translations.literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not remove them.

sentences.zh: 縉云氏有不才子，貪于飲食，冒于貨賄，天下謂之饕餮。

translations.idiomatic: The Jinyun clan added a glutton who gorged on food and wine and stuffed his purse—men called him Glutton.
translations.literal: The Jinyun clan had an unworthy son greedy in drink and food, greedy for bribes; the world called him Glutton.

sentences.zh: 天下惡之，比之三凶。

translations.idiomatic: The people loathed him and classed him with the three fiends.
translations.literal: The world hated them and compared them to the three villains.

sentences.zh: 舜賓於四門，乃流四凶族，遷于四裔，以御螭魅，於是四門辟，言毋凶人也。

translations.idiomatic: When Shun received the lords at the four gates he exiled the four wicked lines to the rim of the world to drive off demons—so the gates stood open and none dared call evil “minister.”
translations.literal: Shun guested at the four gates and thereupon banished these four evil clans, removing them to the four edges to ward off demons and sprites; thus the four gates opened and none said there were evil men.

translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Hong clan bred a villain who smothered right and nursed evil, delighting in cruelty—men named him Chaos. The Shaohao line had a rogue who broke faith and spurned loyalty, trumpeting slander—men called him Wretch. The Zhuanxu clan produced a brute who would not learn or heed counsel—men called him Blockhead. For generations these three houses were the scourge of the age. Even Yao could not uproot them. The Jinyun clan added a glutton who gorged on food and wine and stuffed his purse—men called him Glutton. The people loathed him and classed him with the three fiends. When Shun received the lords at the four gates he exiled the four wicked lines to the rim of the world to drive off demons—so the gates stood open and none dared call evil “minister.”
translations.literal: Formerly the Emperor Hong clan had an unworthy son who hid righteousness and harbored villainy, loving to do violence; the world called him Chaos. The Shaohao clan had an unworthy son who destroyed trust and hated loyalty, glorifying evil speech; the world called him Wretch. The Zhuanxu clan had an unworthy son who could not be taught or instructed, who did not understand good words; the world called him Blockhead. These three families generation after generation were the world’s sorrow. Down to Yao, Yao could not remove them. The Jinyun clan had an unworthy son greedy in drink and food, greedy for bribes; the world called him Glutton. The world hated them and compared them to the three villains. Shun guested at the four gates and thereupon banished these four evil clans, removing them to the four edges to ward off demons and sprites; thus the four gates opened and none said there were evil men.

sentences.zh: 舜入于大麓，烈風雷雨不迷，堯乃知舜之足授天下。

translations.idiomatic: Shun walked the great moor in gales and thunder and never lost his way; Yao knew he could receive the mandate.
translations.literal: Shun entered the great forest; fierce wind, thunder, and rain did not confuse him; Yao then knew Shun was fit to receive all under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 堯老，使舜攝行天子政，巡狩。

translations.idiomatic: When Yao grew feeble he made Shun regent and sent him to tour the realm.
translations.literal: Yao being aged, he made Shun act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and go on tour.

sentences.zh: 舜得舉用事二十年，而堯使攝政。

translations.idiomatic: Twenty years Shun held office before Yao named him regent.
translations.literal: Shun was raised to office twenty years, and Yao made him regent.

sentences.zh: 攝政八年而堯崩。

translations.idiomatic: Eight years as regent, then Yao died.
translations.literal: Eight years he held the regency and Yao died.

sentences.zh: 三年喪畢，讓丹朱，天下歸舜。

translations.idiomatic: After mourning he yielded to Dan Zhu, but the realm rallied to Shun.
translations.literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished he yielded to Dan Zhu, yet all under Heaven turned to Shun.

sentences.zh: 而禹、皋陶、契、后稷、伯夷、夔、龍、倕、益、彭祖自堯時而皆舉用，未有分職。

translations.idiomatic: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, and Peng Zu had served since Yao’s day without fixed portfolios.
translations.literal: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, Peng Zu had all been raised from Yao’s time but had not yet received separate duties.

sentences.zh: 於是舜乃至於文祖，謀于四嶽，辟四門，明通四方耳目，命十二牧論帝德，行厚德，遠佞人，則蠻夷率服。

translations.idiomatic: Shun entered Wenzu, spoke with the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, sharpened sight and hearing across the realm, ordered the twelve pastors to expound Yao’s virtue, spread deep kindness, drive off sycophants—and the wild tribes submitted.
translations.literal: Thereupon Shun went to Wen Temple, consulted the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, cleared the eyes and ears of the four quarters, commanded the twelve pastors to discourse on Emperor Yao’s virtue, to carry deep kindness, to banish flatterers, and then the barbarians all submitted.

sentences.zh: 舜謂四嶽曰：「有能奮庸美堯之事者，使居官相事？」

translations.idiomatic: Shun asked the Four Peaks, “Who can carry on Yao’s work with zeal and serve as minister?”
translations.literal: Shun said to the Four Peaks, “Who can vigorously accomplish Yao’s affairs and may be placed in office to assist?”

sentences.zh: 皆曰：「伯禹為司空，可美帝功。」

translations.idiomatic: All answered, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can finish what Yao began.”
translations.literal: All said, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can complete the emperor’s merit.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「嗟，然！

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “So be it!
translations.literal: Shun said, “Alas, yes!

sentences.zh: 禹，汝平水土，維是勉哉。」

translations.idiomatic: Yu—you must tame the flood; bend every effort to it.”
translations.literal: Yu, you level the waters and earth—only strive at this.”

sentences.zh: 禹拜稽首，讓於稷、契與皋陶。

translations.idiomatic: Yu kowtowed and tried to yield to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.
translations.literal: Yu bowed his head to the ground, yielding to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「然，往矣。」

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you are the man.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Yes, go.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「棄，黎民始饑，汝后稷播時百穀。」

translations.idiomatic: Shun told Hou Ji, “The black-haired people are beginning to hunger—sow the hundred grains in their seasons.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Qi, the black-haired people are beginning to starve; you, Hou Ji, sow in season the hundred grains.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「契，百姓不親，五品不馴，汝為司徒，而敬敷五教，在寬。」

translations.idiomatic: Shun told Qi, “Kinship falters and the five relations waver—be Minister of Education, spread the five teachings with gentle patience.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Qi, the hundred surnames are not intimate, the five grades are not tame; you be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; be lenient.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「皋陶，蠻夷猾夏，寇賊姦軌，汝作士，五刑有服，五服三就；

translations.idiomatic: Shun told Gao Yao, “Barbarians harass the heartland and villains breed—be Minister of Crime; let the five punishments fit the crime, with three degrees of enforcement.
translations.literal: Shun said, “Gao Yao, the outer barbarians disturb the Xia, bandits, thieves, and traitors arise; you be Minister of Crime—the five punishments have their applications, the five applications have three venues.

sentences.zh: 五流有度，五度三居：維明能信。」

translations.idiomatic: Exile has five grades and three dwelling zones—only a clear judge wins trust.”
translations.literal: The five exiles have their measures, the three measures have three dwellings: only enlightenment can keep faith.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「誰能馴予工？」

translations.idiomatic: Shun asked, “Who will master my artisans?”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Who can tame my workmen?”

sentences.zh: 皆曰垂可。

translations.idiomatic: All named Chui.
translations.literal: All said Chui might.

sentences.zh: 於是以垂為共工。

translations.idiomatic: Shun made Chui Minister of Works.
translations.literal: Thereupon he made Chui Minister of Works.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「誰能馴予上下草木鳥獸？」

translations.idiomatic: He asked next, “Who will oversee forests, marshes, birds, and beasts?”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Who can tame for me above and below the grasses and trees, birds and beasts?”

sentences.zh: 皆曰益可。

translations.idiomatic: All named Yi.
translations.literal: All said Yi might.

sentences.zh: 於是以益為朕虞。

translations.idiomatic: He appointed Yi master of the royal preserves.
translations.literal: Thereupon he made Yi Director of the Forbidden Parks.

sentences.zh: 益拜稽首，讓于諸臣朱虎、熊羆。

translations.idiomatic: Yi kowtowed and tried to yield to Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.
translations.literal: Yi bowed his head to the ground, yielding to the ministers Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「往矣，汝諧。」

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you shall work with them.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Go—you harmonize them.”

sentences.zh: 遂以朱虎、熊羆為佐。

translations.idiomatic: He named Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.
translations.literal: Thereupon he used Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「嗟！

translations.idiomatic: Shun cried, “Hear me!
translations.literal: Shun said, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 四嶽，有能典朕三禮？」

translations.idiomatic: O Four Peaks—who will superintend the three great rites?”
translations.literal: You Four Peaks—is there one who can preside over my three rites?”

sentences.zh: 皆曰伯夷可。

translations.idiomatic: All named Boyi.
translations.literal: All said Boyi might.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「嗟！

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “Then,
translations.literal: Shun said, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 伯夷，以汝為秩宗，夙夜維敬，直哉維靜絜。」

translations.idiomatic: Boyi, you shall be Director of the Ancestral Order—watch day and night, upright, calm, and stainless.”
translations.literal: Boyi, I make you Director of the Ancestral Order; morning and night be reverent; be straight, be still, be pure.”

sentences.zh: 伯夷讓夔、龍。

translations.idiomatic: Boyi tried to yield to Kui and Long.
translations.literal: Boyi yielded to Kui and Long.

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「然。

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “Very well.
translations.literal: Shun said, “Yes.

sentences.zh: 以夔為典樂，教稚子，直而溫，寬而栗，剛而毋虐，簡而毋傲；

translations.idiomatic: Kui shall be Director of Music to train the heirs—straight yet kind, broad yet disciplined, stern without cruelty, plain without arrogance.
translations.literal: I make Kui Director of Music to teach the noble sons—straight yet warm, wide yet firm, hard without cruelty, simple without arrogance.

sentences.zh: 詩言意，歌長言，聲依永，律和聲，八音能諧，毋相奪倫，神人以和。」

translations.idiomatic: Poetry voices the mind, song draws out the words, melody follows the drawn tones, pitch rules the melody—when the eight timbres blend without usurping one another, gods and men are reconciled.”
translations.literal: Poetry speaks the intent, song prolongs the speech, notes rely on the prolongation, pitchpipes harmonize the notes; when the eight kinds of sound harmonize and none seize one another’s order, spirits and men are at peace.”

sentences.zh: 夔曰：「於！

translations.idiomatic: Kui answered, “Ah!
translations.literal: Kui said, “Oh!

sentences.zh: 予擊石拊石，百獸率舞。」

translations.idiomatic: When I strike and tap the sounding-stone, the hundred beasts all fall into step.”
translations.literal: I strike the stone and pat the stone and the hundred beasts lead the dance.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「龍，朕畏忌讒說殄偽，振驚朕眾，命汝為納言，夙夜出入朕命，惟信。」

translations.idiomatic: Shun told Long, “Slander and deceit would shake my people—you shall be chief of reception; issue and record my orders day and night with perfect good faith.”
translations.literal: Shun said, “Long, I fear slanderous words and treacherous deeds that alarm my hosts; I command you to be the mouth of reception—morning and night issue and receive my commands—only trustworthiness.”

sentences.zh: 舜曰：「嗟！

translations.idiomatic: Shun said, “You
translations.literal: Shun said, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 女二十有二人，敬哉，惟時相天事。」

translations.idiomatic: twenty-two—be vigilant; each in season help heaven’s work.”
translations.literal: you twenty-two men, be reverent—each in his season assist Heaven’s affairs.”

sentences.zh: 三歲一考功，三考絀陟，遠近眾功咸興。

translations.idiomatic: Every third year he reviewed their deeds; after three reviews the unworthy fell and the worthy rose, and every task near or far throve.
translations.literal: Every three years they examined merit; after three examinations demotions and promotions were settled, and near and far all tasks flourished.

sentences.zh: 分北三苗。

translations.idiomatic: He broke the power of the Three Miao and drove them north.
translations.literal: He divided and sent north the Three Miao.

translations.idiomatic: Shun walked the great moor in gales and thunder and never lost his way; Yao knew he could receive the mandate. When Yao grew feeble he made Shun regent and sent him to tour the realm. Twenty years Shun held office before Yao named him regent. Eight years as regent, then Yao died. After mourning he yielded to Dan Zhu, but the realm rallied to Shun. Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, and Peng Zu had served since Yao’s day without fixed portfolios. Shun entered Wenzu, spoke with the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, sharpened sight and hearing across the realm, ordered the twelve pastors to expound Yao’s virtue, spread deep kindness, drive off sycophants—and the wild tribes submitted. Shun asked the Four Peaks, “Who can carry on Yao’s work with zeal and serve as minister?” All answered, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can finish what Yao began.” Shun said, “So be it! Yu—you must tame the flood; bend every effort to it.” Yu kowtowed and tried to yield to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao. Shun said, “Go—you are the man.” Shun told Hou Ji, “The black-haired people are beginning to hunger—sow the hundred grains in their seasons.” Shun told Qi, “Kinship falters and the five relations waver—be Minister of Education, spread the five teachings with gentle patience.” Shun told Gao Yao, “Barbarians harass the heartland and villains breed—be Minister of Crime; let the five punishments fit the crime, with three degrees of enforcement. Exile has five grades and three dwelling zones—only a clear judge wins trust.” Shun asked, “Who will master my artisans?” All named Chui. Shun made Chui Minister of Works. He asked next, “Who will oversee forests, marshes, birds, and beasts?” All named Yi. He appointed Yi master of the royal preserves. Yi kowtowed and tried to yield to Zhu Hu and Xiongpi. Shun said, “Go—you shall work with them.” He named Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants. Shun cried, “Hear me! O Four Peaks—who will superintend the three great rites?” All named Boyi. Shun said, “Then, Boyi, you shall be Director of the Ancestral Order—watch day and night, upright, calm, and stainless.” Boyi tried to yield to Kui and Long. Shun said, “Very well. Kui shall be Director of Music to train the heirs—straight yet kind, broad yet disciplined, stern without cruelty, plain without arrogance. Poetry voices the mind, song draws out the words, melody follows the drawn tones, pitch rules the melody—when the eight timbres blend without usurping one another, gods and men are reconciled.” Kui answered, “Ah! When I strike and tap the sounding-stone, the hundred beasts all fall into step.” Shun told Long, “Slander and deceit would shake my people—you shall be chief of reception; issue and record my orders day and night with perfect good faith.” Shun said, “You twenty-two—be vigilant; each in season help heaven’s work.” Every third year he reviewed their deeds; after three reviews the unworthy fell and the worthy rose, and every task near or far throve. He broke the power of the Three Miao and drove them north.
translations.literal: Shun entered the great forest; fierce wind, thunder, and rain did not confuse him; Yao then knew Shun was fit to receive all under Heaven. Yao being aged, he made Shun act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and go on tour. Shun was raised to office twenty years, and Yao made him regent. Eight years he held the regency and Yao died. When the three years’ mourning were finished he yielded to Dan Zhu, yet all under Heaven turned to Shun. Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, Peng Zu had all been raised from Yao’s time but had not yet received separate duties. Thereupon Shun went to Wen Temple, consulted the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, cleared the eyes and ears of the four quarters, commanded the twelve pastors to discourse on Emperor Yao’s virtue, to carry deep kindness, to banish flatterers, and then the barbarians all submitted. Shun said to the Four Peaks, “Who can vigorously accomplish Yao’s affairs and may be placed in office to assist?” All said, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can complete the emperor’s merit.” Shun said, “Alas, yes! Yu, you level the waters and earth—only strive at this.” Yu bowed his head to the ground, yielding to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao. Shun said, “Yes, go.” Shun said, “Qi, the black-haired people are beginning to starve; you, Hou Ji, sow in season the hundred grains.” Shun said, “Qi, the hundred surnames are not intimate, the five grades are not tame; you be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; be lenient.” Shun said, “Gao Yao, the outer barbarians disturb the Xia, bandits, thieves, and traitors arise; you be Minister of Crime—the five punishments have their applications, the five applications have three venues. The five exiles have their measures, the three measures have three dwellings: only enlightenment can keep faith.” Shun said, “Who can tame my workmen?” All said Chui might. Thereupon he made Chui Minister of Works. Shun said, “Who can tame for me above and below the grasses and trees, birds and beasts?” All said Yi might. Thereupon he made Yi Director of the Forbidden Parks. Yi bowed his head to the ground, yielding to the ministers Zhu Hu and Xiongpi. Shun said, “Go—you harmonize them.” Thereupon he used Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants. Shun said, “Alas! You Four Peaks—is there one who can preside over my three rites?” All said Boyi might. Shun said, “Alas! Boyi, I make you Director of the Ancestral Order; morning and night be reverent; be straight, be still, be pure.” Boyi yielded to Kui and Long. Shun said, “Yes. I make Kui Director of Music to teach the noble sons—straight yet warm, wide yet firm, hard without cruelty, simple without arrogance. Poetry speaks the intent, song prolongs the speech, notes rely on the prolongation, pitchpipes harmonize the notes; when the eight kinds of sound harmonize and none seize one another’s order, spirits and men are at peace.” Kui said, “Oh! I strike the stone and pat the stone and the hundred beasts lead the dance.” Shun said, “Long, I fear slanderous words and treacherous deeds that alarm my hosts; I command you to be the mouth of reception—morning and night issue and receive my commands—only trustworthiness.” Shun said, “Alas! you twenty-two men, be reverent—each in his season assist Heaven’s affairs.” Every three years they examined merit; after three examinations demotions and promotions were settled, and near and far all tasks flourished. He divided and sent north the Three Miao.

sentences.zh: 此二十二人咸成厥功：皋陶為大理，平，民各伏得其實；

translations.idiomatic: Those twenty-two all proved their worth: Gao Yao as judge was even-handed and the people trusted his verdicts.
translations.literal: These twenty-two men all completed their merit: Gao Yao as chief judge was fair and the people each submitted to the truth.

sentences.zh: 伯夷主禮，上下咸讓；

translations.idiomatic: Boyi kept the rites and high and low deferred with grace.
translations.literal: Boyi presided over the rites and above and below showed yielding courtesy.

sentences.zh: 垂主工師，百工致功；

translations.idiomatic: Chui as minister of works made every craft succeed.
translations.literal: Chui as master of workmen made the hundred crafts achieve their work.

sentences.zh: 益主虞，山澤辟；

translations.idiomatic: Yi as warden opened hills and marshes.
translations.literal: Yi as park director opened hills and marshes.

sentences.zh: 棄主稷，百穀時茂；

translations.idiomatic: Qi as lord of grain made every crop thrive in its season.
translations.literal: Qi as lord of grain made the hundred grains flourish in season.

sentences.zh: 契主司徒，百姓親和；

translations.idiomatic: Qi as minister of education knit the clans in harmony.
translations.literal: Qi as Minister of Education made the hundred surnames intimate and harmonious.

sentences.zh: 龍主賓客，遠人至；

translations.idiomatic: Long as master of guests drew far peoples in.
translations.literal: Long as guest director brought distant men to court.

sentences.zh: 十二牧行而九州莫敢辟違；

translations.idiomatic: The twelve pastors rode circuit and none in the nine provinces dared defy them.
translations.literal: The twelve pastors traveled and within the nine provinces none dared to shirk or rebel.

sentences.zh: 唯禹之功為大，披九山，通九澤，決九河，定九州，各以其職來貢，不失厥宜。

translations.idiomatic: Yet Yu’s work towered above the rest—he cut passes through nine ranges, channeled nine swamps, dredged nine rivers, and settled nine provinces, each paying tribute in kind without fault.
translations.literal: Only Yu’s merit was greatest: he opened the nine mountains, linked the nine marshes, dredged the nine rivers, fixed the nine provinces; each region offered tribute according to its office and never missed what was proper.

sentences.zh: 方五千里，至于荒服。

translations.idiomatic: His domain ran five thousand li square to the wild frontier.
translations.literal: Five thousand li formed a square to the wild border.

sentences.zh: 南撫交阯、北發，西戎、析枝、渠廋、氐、羌，北山戎、發、息慎，東長、鳥夷，四海之內咸戴帝舜之功。

translations.idiomatic: South to Jiaozhi and Beifa, west to the Rong tribes, north to Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen, east to the long coast and bird Yi—all within the seas praised Shun’s virtue.
translations.literal: South he soothed Jiaozhi, Beifa, western Rong, Zhizhi, Qusou, Di, and Qiang; north Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen; east Chang and the bird-barbarians: within the four seas they all bore the virtue of Emperor Shun.

sentences.zh: 於是禹乃興九招之樂，致異物，鳳皇來翔。

translations.idiomatic: Yu then performed the Nine Summons; prodigies appeared and phoenixes wheeled overhead.
translations.literal: Thereupon Yu raised the music of the Nine Summons; strange creatures came; phoenixes came and circled.

sentences.zh: 天下明德皆自虞帝始。

translations.idiomatic: The luminous virtue that spread through the realm began with Emperor Shun of Yu.
translations.literal: All under Heaven’s bright virtue began from the Yu Emperor (Shun).

translations.idiomatic: Those twenty-two all proved their worth: Gao Yao as judge was even-handed and the people trusted his verdicts. Boyi kept the rites and high and low deferred with grace. Chui as minister of works made every craft succeed. Yi as warden opened hills and marshes. Qi as lord of grain made every crop thrive in its season. Qi as minister of education knit the clans in harmony. Long as master of guests drew far peoples in. The twelve pastors rode circuit and none in the nine provinces dared defy them. Yet Yu’s work towered above the rest—he cut passes through nine ranges, channeled nine swamps, dredged nine rivers, and settled nine provinces, each paying tribute in kind without fault. His domain ran five thousand li square to the wild frontier. South to Jiaozhi and Beifa, west to the Rong tribes, north to Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen, east to the long coast and bird Yi—all within the seas praised Shun’s virtue. Yu then performed the Nine Summons; prodigies appeared and phoenixes wheeled overhead. The luminous virtue that spread through the realm began with Emperor Shun of Yu.
translations.literal: These twenty-two men all completed their merit: Gao Yao as chief judge was fair and the people each submitted to the truth. Boyi presided over the rites and above and below showed yielding courtesy. Chui as master of workmen made the hundred crafts achieve their work. Yi as park director opened hills and marshes. Qi as lord of grain made the hundred grains flourish in season. Qi as Minister of Education made the hundred surnames intimate and harmonious. Long as guest director brought distant men to court. The twelve pastors traveled and within the nine provinces none dared to shirk or rebel. Only Yu’s merit was greatest: he opened the nine mountains, linked the nine marshes, dredged the nine rivers, fixed the nine provinces; each region offered tribute according to its office and never missed what was proper. Five thousand li formed a square to the wild border. South he soothed Jiaozhi, Beifa, western Rong, Zhizhi, Qusou, Di, and Qiang; north Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen; east Chang and the bird-barbarians: within the four seas they all bore the virtue of Emperor Shun. Thereupon Yu raised the music of the Nine Summons; strange creatures came; phoenixes came and circled. All under Heaven’s bright virtue began from the Yu Emperor (Shun).

sentences.zh: 舜年二十以孝聞，年三十堯舉之，年五十攝行天子事，年五十八堯崩，年六十一代堯踐帝位。

translations.idiomatic: Famed for filial piety at twenty, chosen at thirty, regent at fifty, he mourned Yao’s death at fifty-eight, and at sixty-one inherited the throne.
translations.literal: Shun at twenty was famed for filial piety; at thirty Yao raised him; at fifty he acted as regent; at fifty-eight Yao died; at sixty-one he replaced Yao and mounted the imperial seat.

sentences.zh: 踐帝位三十九年，南巡狩，崩於蒼梧之野。

translations.idiomatic: Thirty-nine years he reigned, then died on his southern tour in the wilds of Cangwu.
translations.literal: Thirty-nine years on the throne he toured south and died in the wilds of Cangwu.

sentences.zh: 葬於江南九疑，是為零陵。

translations.idiomatic: He was buried south of the river at Jiuyi—what men call Lingling.
translations.literal: He was buried south of the Yangzi at Jiuyi; this is called Lingling.

sentences.zh: 舜之踐帝位，載天子旗，往朝父瞽叟，夔夔唯謹，如子道。

translations.idiomatic: On taking the throne he rode with imperial pennons to visit blind Gu Sou, trembling with the awe a son owes a father.
translations.literal: When Shun mounted the throne he flew the Son of Heaven’s banners and went to court on his father Gu Sou, trembling in utmost reverence as a son’s way requires.

sentences.zh: 封弟象為諸侯。

translations.idiomatic: He ennobled his brother Xiang as a lord.
translations.literal: He enfeoffed his younger brother Xiang as a feudal lord.

sentences.zh: 舜子商均亦不肖，舜乃豫薦禹於天。

translations.idiomatic: His son Shangjun proved unworthy, so Shun long before had commended Yu to heaven.
translations.literal: Shun’s son Shangjun was also not worthy; Shun therefore beforehand commended Yu to Heaven.

sentences.zh: 十七年而崩。

translations.idiomatic: Seventeen years he reigned after that, then died.
translations.literal: Seventeen years and he died.

sentences.zh: 三年喪畢，禹亦乃讓舜子，如舜讓堯子。

translations.idiomatic: After mourning Yu yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had once yielded to Yao’s son.
translations.literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished, Yu likewise yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had yielded to Yao’s son.

sentences.zh: 諸侯歸之，然後禹踐天子位。

translations.idiomatic: The lords rallied to Yu, and he then took the throne.
translations.literal: The feudal lords turned to him, and afterward Yu mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat.

sentences.zh: 堯子丹朱，舜子商均，皆有疆土，以奉先祀。

translations.idiomatic: Dan Zhu and Shangjun kept fiefs to tend their fathers’ shrines.
translations.literal: Yao’s son Dan Zhu and Shun’s son Shangjun each had territory allotted to sacrifice to their ancestors.

sentences.zh: 服其服，禮樂如之。

translations.idiomatic: They wore their fathers’ vestments and kept their fathers’ music and rites.
translations.literal: They wore their fathers’ robes and used their music and rites.

sentences.zh: 以客見天子，天子弗臣，示不敢專也。

translations.idiomatic: The reigning king received them as guests, not vassals, to show the throne was never a private possession.
translations.literal: As guests they were received by the Son of Heaven, who did not treat them as subjects, showing he dared not monopolize the realm.

translations.idiomatic: Famed for filial piety at twenty, chosen at thirty, regent at fifty, he mourned Yao’s death at fifty-eight, and at sixty-one inherited the throne. Thirty-nine years he reigned, then died on his southern tour in the wilds of Cangwu. He was buried south of the river at Jiuyi—what men call Lingling. On taking the throne he rode with imperial pennons to visit blind Gu Sou, trembling with the awe a son owes a father. He ennobled his brother Xiang as a lord. His son Shangjun proved unworthy, so Shun long before had commended Yu to heaven. Seventeen years he reigned after that, then died. After mourning Yu yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had once yielded to Yao’s son. The lords rallied to Yu, and he then took the throne. Dan Zhu and Shangjun kept fiefs to tend their fathers’ shrines. They wore their fathers’ vestments and kept their fathers’ music and rites. The reigning king received them as guests, not vassals, to show the throne was never a private possession.
translations.literal: Shun at twenty was famed for filial piety; at thirty Yao raised him; at fifty he acted as regent; at fifty-eight Yao died; at sixty-one he replaced Yao and mounted the imperial seat. Thirty-nine years on the throne he toured south and died in the wilds of Cangwu. He was buried south of the Yangzi at Jiuyi; this is called Lingling. When Shun mounted the throne he flew the Son of Heaven’s banners and went to court on his father Gu Sou, trembling in utmost reverence as a son’s way requires. He enfeoffed his younger brother Xiang as a feudal lord. Shun’s son Shangjun was also not worthy; Shun therefore beforehand commended Yu to Heaven. Seventeen years and he died. When the three years’ mourning were finished, Yu likewise yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had yielded to Yao’s son. The feudal lords turned to him, and afterward Yu mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat. Yao’s son Dan Zhu and Shun’s son Shangjun each had territory allotted to sacrifice to their ancestors. They wore their fathers’ robes and used their music and rites. As guests they were received by the Son of Heaven, who did not treat them as subjects, showing he dared not monopolize the realm.

sentences.zh: 自黃帝至舜、禹，皆同姓而異其國號，以章明德。

translations.idiomatic: From Huangdi through Shun and Yu the line shared one surname yet changed dynastic titles to mark their shining virtue.
translations.literal: From the Yellow Emperor to Shun and Yu all shared the same surname but changed their state names to display their bright virtue.

sentences.zh: 故黃帝為有熊，帝顓頊為高陽，帝嚳為高辛，帝堯為陶唐，帝舜為有虞。

translations.idiomatic: Thus Huangdi styled his house Youxiong, Zhuanxu Gaoyang, Ku Gaoxin, Yao Taotang, Shun Youyu.
translations.literal: Therefore the Yellow Emperor was Youxiong, Emperor Zhuanxu was Gaoyang, Emperor Ku was Gaoxin, Emperor Yao was Taotang, Emperor Shun was Youyu.

sentences.zh: 帝禹為夏后而別氏，姓姒氏。

translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yu founded Xia and took the clan name Si.
translations.literal: Emperor Yu was lord of Xia and took a separate surname, the Si clan.

sentences.zh: 契為商，姓子氏。

translations.idiomatic: Qi founded Shang with the clan name Zi.
translations.literal: Qi was lord of Shang, surname Zi.

sentences.zh: 棄為周，姓姬氏。

translations.idiomatic: Qi founded Zhou with the clan name Ji.
translations.literal: Qi was lord of Zhou, surname Ji.

translations.idiomatic: From Huangdi through Shun and Yu the line shared one surname yet changed dynastic titles to mark their shining virtue. Thus Huangdi styled his house Youxiong, Zhuanxu Gaoyang, Ku Gaoxin, Yao Taotang, Shun Youyu. Emperor Yu founded Xia and took the clan name Si. Qi founded Shang with the clan name Zi. Qi founded Zhou with the clan name Ji.
translations.literal: From the Yellow Emperor to Shun and Yu all shared the same surname but changed their state names to display their bright virtue. Therefore the Yellow Emperor was Youxiong, Emperor Zhuanxu was Gaoyang, Emperor Ku was Gaoxin, Emperor Yao was Taotang, Emperor Shun was Youyu. Emperor Yu was lord of Xia and took a separate surname, the Si clan. Qi was lord of Shang, surname Zi. Qi was lord of Zhou, surname Ji.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：學者多稱五帝，尚矣。

translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Learned men praise the Five Emperors—yet their age lies far in the past.
translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: Scholars for the most part praise the Five Emperors—this is ancient indeed.

sentences.zh: 然尚書獨載堯以來；

translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Documents begins only with Yao.
translations.literal: Yet the Document only records what comes from Yao onward.

sentences.zh: 而百家言黃帝，其文不雅馴，薦紳先生難言之。

translations.idiomatic: The hundred philosophers tell of the Yellow Emperor, yet their language is coarse and doubtful, which gentlemen seldom repeat.
translations.literal: The hundred schools speak of the Yellow Emperor, but their texts are not elegant or trustworthy, and gentry scholars hesitate to repeat them.

sentences.zh: 孔子所傳宰予問五帝德及帝系姓，儒者或不傳。

translations.idiomatic: Confucius handed down Zai Wo’s “Virtues of the Five Emperors” and the “Imperial Genealogies,” yet many Ruists decline to teach them.
translations.literal: What Confucius transmitted—Zai Wo’s questions on the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames—some Confucians do not pass on.

sentences.zh: 余嘗西至空桐，北過涿鹿，東漸於海，南浮江淮矣，至長老皆各往往稱黃帝、堯、舜之處，風教固殊焉，總之不離古文者近是。

translations.idiomatic: I have ridden west to Hollow Tong, north beyond Zhuolu, east to the sea, and south along the Yangzi and Huai; everywhere elders still pointed out where Huangdi, Yao, and Shun dwelt, though local lore varied—yet what agrees with the old documents is likeliest true.
translations.literal: I have traveled west to Hollow Tong, north past Zhuolu, east to the sea, south along the Yangzi and Huai; elders in each place often pointed out the haunts of the Yellow Emperor, Yao, and Shun, yet their customs and teachings differed; in sum, what does not stray from the ancient texts is near the truth.

sentences.zh: 予觀春秋、國語，其發明五帝德、帝系姓章矣，顧弟弗深考，其所表見皆不虛。

translations.idiomatic: Reading the Spring and Autumn Annals and Discourses of the States, I find their accounts of the Five Emperors’ virtue and the royal genealogies lucid, though scholars rarely study them in depth; what they record is not idle tale.
translations.literal: I examined the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Discourses of the States; they develop the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames quite clearly, though scholars have not dug deeply—their testimony is not empty.

sentences.zh: 書缺有閒矣，其軼乃時時見於他說。

translations.idiomatic: The Documents have lacunae; stray passages surface in other works.
translations.literal: The Documents have gaps; their scattered fragments appear in other traditions.

sentences.zh: 非好學深思，心知其意，固難為淺見寡聞道也。

translations.idiomatic: Without fond learning and deep thought to seize their sense, one cannot expound them for the shallow-minded.
translations.literal: Unless one loves learning and thinks deeply to grasp their meaning, it is hard to explain them to shallow views and narrow hearing.

sentences.zh: 余并論次，擇其言尤雅者，故著為本紀書首。

translations.idiomatic: I have ordered and selected the most polished accounts, and therefore place them at the opening of the annals.
translations.literal: I have arranged and discussed them, choosing the most elegant language, therefore I set them at the head of the basic annals.

translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Learned men praise the Five Emperors—yet their age lies far in the past. The Classic of Documents begins only with Yao. The hundred philosophers tell of the Yellow Emperor, yet their language is coarse and doubtful, which gentlemen seldom repeat. Confucius handed down Zai Wo’s “Virtues of the Five Emperors” and the “Imperial Genealogies,” yet many Ruists decline to teach them. I have ridden west to Hollow Tong, north beyond Zhuolu, east to the sea, and south along the Yangzi and Huai; everywhere elders still pointed out where Huangdi, Yao, and Shun dwelt, though local lore varied—yet what agrees with the old documents is likeliest true. Reading the Spring and Autumn Annals and Discourses of the States, I find their accounts of the Five Emperors’ virtue and the royal genealogies lucid, though scholars rarely study them in depth; what they record is not idle tale. The Documents have lacunae; stray passages surface in other works. Without fond learning and deep thought to seize their sense, one cannot expound them for the shallow-minded. I have ordered and selected the most polished accounts, and therefore place them at the opening of the annals.
translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: Scholars for the most part praise the Five Emperors—this is ancient indeed. Yet the Document only records what comes from Yao onward. The hundred schools speak of the Yellow Emperor, but their texts are not elegant or trustworthy, and gentry scholars hesitate to repeat them. What Confucius transmitted—Zai Wo’s questions on the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames—some Confucians do not pass on. I have traveled west to Hollow Tong, north past Zhuolu, east to the sea, south along the Yangzi and Huai; elders in each place often pointed out the haunts of the Yellow Emperor, Yao, and Shun, yet their customs and teachings differed; in sum, what does not stray from the ancient texts is near the truth. I examined the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Discourses of the States; they develop the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames quite clearly, though scholars have not dug deeply—their testimony is not empty. The Documents have gaps; their scattered fragments appear in other traditions. Unless one loves learning and thinks deeply to grasp their meaning, it is hard to explain them to shallow views and narrow hearing. I have arranged and discussed them, choosing the most elegant language, therefore I set them at the head of the basic annals.
